title: A BOM revision records what changed (line, qty, alternate)
goal:
Verify that creating a BOM revision logs the change in detail —
which lines, what changed, prior values, new values, and the actor.
preconditions: A finished part has a released BOM (P2-BOM-001).

steps.action:
Open the BOM. Change one component quantity. Add an alternate.
Save.
steps.expected: A new revision is created.

steps.action: Open the BOM history / audit log for this part.
steps.expected:
The revision entry shows: actor, timestamp, the prior
revision's line values, the new revision's line values, and
a summary of what changed.

expected_overall: BOM revisions surface a real diff, not just a "modified" flag.
pass_criteria: Revision history shows changed lines with before/after values.

title: Deactivating a customer, vendor, or user records actor and target
goal:
Verify deactivating any of the three entities (customer, vendor,
user) records the action in the audit log.
preconditions: At least one customer, one vendor, and one user are in a state where they can be deactivated.

steps.action:
Deactivate one of each entity (per existing P1-USER-003,
P2-VENDOR-005, and the equivalent customer flow).
steps.expected: All three deactivations succeed.

steps.action: Open the audit log filtered to deactivation events.
steps.expected:
Three entries are present, each with actor, target entity,
target ID, and timestamp.

expected_overall: Deactivations are uniformly audited across entity types.
pass_criteria: All three deactivations recorded with full attribution.

title: Audit entries are immutable; no role can edit or delete them
goal:
Verify the audit log is append-only — no role, including
Administrator, can edit an existing entry, delete an entry, or
alter timestamps.
preconditions: At least 10 audit log entries exist from prior cases.

steps.action:
As Administrator, open the audit log. Look for any edit, delete,
or modify action on an individual entry.
steps.expected: No such action exists in the UI.

steps.action:
If the application has an API, attempt to PATCH or DELETE an
audit log entry.
steps.expected: The request is rejected. The entry is unchanged.

steps.action: Reload the audit log.
steps.expected: Every entry from before the test is still present unchanged.

expected_overall: Audit log is genuinely immutable.
pass_criteria:
No edit / delete possible from UI AND API rejects modification AND
count of entries is unchanged.
why_this_matters:
Editable audit logs are not audit logs. The entire point is that
what was recorded cannot be quietly altered after the fact.

title: Successful and failed sign-in events are recorded with source detail
goal:
Verify that sign-in successes, sign-in failures, and sign-outs are
all recorded with user, timestamp, IP / source, and outcome.
preconditions: At least two users exist.
The audit log is reachable for the IT Admin.

steps.action: As IT Admin, sign out. Sign back in successfully.
steps.expected: Sign-in succeeds.

steps.action:
Sign out, then attempt to sign in with a deliberately wrong
password. Then sign in correctly.
steps.expected: Failed attempt, then successful sign-in.

steps.action: Open the audit log filtered to authentication events.
steps.expected:
Three entries are visible: a sign-out, a failed sign-in attempt,
a successful sign-in. Each has user (or attempted user),
timestamp, source IP / device, and outcome.

expected_overall: Authentication events are fully recorded.
pass_criteria:
All three events present in the audit log AND failure is
distinguishable from success AND source detail is captured.

title: Closing and reopening a fiscal period are both logged
goal:
Verify the close-period action and any subsequent reopen action
both appear in the audit log with actor, period, timestamp, and
reason (if provided).
preconditions: A Controller user exists.
At least one period is in a state ready to close.

steps.action: Close the period (per P5-CLOSE-004).
steps.expected: Period closes.

steps.action: Reopen the period (per P5-CLOSE-004-N1) with a reason.
steps.expected: Period reopens.

steps.action: Open the audit log.
steps.expected:
Both events present: close (actor, period, timestamp) and reopen
(actor, period, timestamp, reason).

expected_overall: Period state changes are fully audited including reasons on reopen.
pass_criteria: Both events present AND reopen records the reason text.

title: Changing the permissions on a role is logged with diff
goal:
Verify that adding or removing a permission from a role records
the change in the audit log with actor, timestamp, role, and a
diff of permissions.
preconditions: At least one custom role exists (P0-USER-001).

steps.action: Open the role. Add a new permission. Save.
steps.expected: Change saves.

steps.action: Remove a different permission. Save.
steps.expected: Change saves.

steps.action: Open the role's audit log.
steps.expected:
Two entries present, each showing: actor, timestamp, role, and
what was added or removed.

expected_overall: Role-permission changes are fully audited.
pass_criteria: Both entries present with full diffs.
why_this_matters:
Permission creep — quietly adding capabilities to roles over time
— is how access control degrades. Without a clear log, no one
notices until a security review.

title: PO amendments are versioned with the original preserved
goal:
Verify that amending an issued PO produces a new version while the
original remains visible in the audit history, with a clear
before/after diff.
preconditions: At least one issued PO exists.

steps.action: Open an issued PO. Amend a line quantity. Save.
steps.expected: Amendment is recorded as a new version.

steps.action: Open the PO history.
steps.expected:
Original version and amended version both visible. Diff shows
the changed line with prior and new quantity. Actor and timestamp
attached to the amendment.

expected_overall: PO history is complete and immutable for prior versions.
pass_criteria: Both versions visible AND diff is clear AND actor / timestamp recorded.

title: Quote or order price override is logged with before and after
goal:
Verify a Sales user's price override on a quote line shows in the
audit log with prior price, new price, actor, and timestamp.
preconditions: A Sales user with override permission exists.
At least one part with a default price exists.

steps.action:
As Sales, create a draft quote line. Override the unit price.
Save.
steps.expected: Override accepted.

steps.action: Open the quote / line audit log.
steps.expected:
Override is recorded with prior price, new price, actor,
timestamp.

expected_overall: Pricing overrides surface for management review.
pass_criteria: Override entry present AND captures all four fields.

title: Changing a user's role is logged with before and after state
goal:
Verify that when an IT Admin changes a user's role, the audit log
shows actor, target user, prior role, new role, and timestamp.
preconditions: An IT Admin user exists.
At least one non-admin user exists.

steps.action:
As IT Admin, open a non-admin user. Note their current role.
Change their role to a different role. Save.
steps.expected: Change saves.

steps.action: Open the audit log filtered to user-management events.
steps.expected:
A role-change entry is present with actor (IT Admin), target
user, prior role, new role, and timestamp.

steps.action: Revert the change.
steps.expected:
Second entry recorded for the revert, also with full before /
after.

expected_overall: User-role changes record full before/after with attribution.
pass_criteria:
Both entries present AND each captures actor, target, prior, new,
timestamp.

title: Bulk-deactivate customers with no activity in N years
goal:
Verify a controller can deactivate a batch of inactive customers
in one action, that customers with open balances or orders are
excluded automatically, and that the operation reports per-customer
outcome.
preconditions: At least 5 customers exist; at least 2 have no activity in the last 3 years; at least 1 has an open invoice.

steps.action:
Find a "customers with no activity in 3 years" filter or
search. Include the customer with an open invoice deliberately.
steps.expected: Filter returns the relevant set.

steps.action: Choose bulk-deactivate. Confirm.
steps.expected:
Result summary: customers with no balance deactivated. Customer
with open invoice rejected with reason "Open AR balance —
cannot deactivate."

steps.action: Try to create an SO for one of the deactivated customers.
steps.expected: Selection blocked or disabled.

expected_overall: Bulk deactivate respects business-rule guardrails.
pass_criteria:
Eligible customers deactivated AND ineligible flagged AND no silent
partial outcome.

title: Reassign a list of customers to a new account manager
goal:
Verify an admin or sales lead can mass-reassign a set of customers
to a different account manager, the change is audit-logged per
customer, and the originating manager loses ownership cleanly.
preconditions: At least 5 customers exist with one account manager assigned.
A second sales user exists.

steps.action:
Filter customer list to those owned by Manager A. Select 3.
Choose mass-reassign. Set new owner = Manager B.
steps.expected: Action stages preview.

steps.action: Confirm.
steps.expected: All 3 reassigned. Each customer's owner = Manager B.

steps.action:
Sign in as Manager A and as Manager B. Verify their respective
customer lists.
steps.expected: A no longer sees the 3 customers. B does.

expected_overall: Reassignment is clean and audited.
pass_criteria:
Ownership changed AND visible in both managers' lists correctly AND
audited.

title: Bulk-update payment terms across a selected set of vendors
goal:
Verify a Procurement user can select multiple vendors from the
list and mass-update payment terms in a single action, with a
per-row success / failure report.
preconditions: At least 5 vendors exist.

steps.action:
Open the vendor list. Filter or select 3 vendors. Choose the
mass-update action. Set payment terms = "Net 45."
steps.expected: Action stages with a preview of the 3 affected records.

steps.action: Confirm.
steps.expected:
Update completes. A summary shows 3 vendors updated. Each
vendor's terms = Net 45 in the list.

steps.action: Verify each vendor's audit log records the change.
steps.expected: All 3 audited with prior / new terms.

expected_overall: Bulk update applies cleanly with audit per record.
pass_criteria:
All 3 vendors updated AND audit captured per vendor AND no other
vendors affected.

title: Bulk-close work orders that have completed all operations
goal:
Verify production management can mass-close a list of completed-
but-not-closed WOs, the operation reports per-WO outcome, and any
WOs that fail to close (e.g., variance reason missing) are flagged
rather than silently skipped.
preconditions: At least 5 WOs exist with all operations complete.
At least one of them has a missing variance reason that should block close (per P4-WOVAR-001).

steps.action:
Filter the WO list to "completed, not closed." Select all.
Choose bulk-close.
steps.expected: Action stages with a per-WO preview.

steps.action: Confirm.
steps.expected:
Result summary: 4 closed, 1 rejected with reason "Variance
reason required." Closed WOs are marked closed; the rejected
one remains open and surfaces what to fix.

steps.action:
Fix the rejected WO's variance reason. Re-run the bulk-close
on it alone.
steps.expected: Now closes successfully.

expected_overall: Bulk close reports per-WO outcomes and never silently fails.
pass_criteria:
Successful WOs closed AND failed WO flagged with reason AND no
silent skips.

title: Two users editing the same customer surfaces a conflict before silent overwrite
goal:
Verify that when two users open the same customer record, edit
different fields, and save, the second save either merges or
surfaces a conflict — it does NOT silently overwrite the first
user's change.
preconditions: Two users with edit access to the same customer exist.
At least one customer record exists.
notes:
Run with two browser windows / tabs side by side. Label them
"User A" and "User B" so the steps stay clear.

steps.action:
User A: open customer ACME Industrial. Note current values for
"phone" and "primary contact name."
steps.expected: Form is editable.

steps.action: User B: in a separate session, open the same customer record.
steps.expected: Form is editable for User B as well.

steps.action: User A: change the phone number. Save.
steps.expected: Save succeeds. Customer now reflects A's change.

steps.action: User B: change the primary contact name (different field). Save.
steps.expected:
The application either:
(a) merges B's change with A's earlier change (both fields
    reflect their respective edits), OR
(b) blocks B's save with a conflict message that includes A's
    change and asks B to confirm.

What is NOT acceptable: B's save silently overwrites A's
change without warning.

steps.action: Reload both sessions. Compare the saved record.
steps.expected:
Both A's and B's changes are reflected (if merged) OR only the
change User B explicitly confirmed is reflected (if conflict
was surfaced).

expected_overall: Concurrent edits do not silently lose work.
pass_criteria:
No silent overwrite occurred AND the system either merged or
surfaced a conflict.
why_this_matters:
Last-write-wins on shared records loses data invisibly. Users do
not notice until much later, when "I know I changed that" turns
out to be true.

title: Saving an edited record from a stale view is rejected with a recoverable error
goal:
Verify that if User A loads a record, User B updates it and saves,
and then User A tries to save their stale copy, User A's save is
rejected with an error that explains the record changed and offers
a way to reload and reapply.
preconditions: At least one record (a customer or part is fine) is editable by both users.

steps.action: User A: open the record. Begin editing a field but do not save.
steps.expected: Form open with stale snapshot in memory.

steps.action:
User B: in a separate session, open and save a change to the
same record.
steps.expected: User B's save succeeds.

steps.action: User A: complete the edit and click save.
steps.expected:
Save is rejected with a clear, plain-English message: the
record was updated by another user since A loaded it. The
message includes either a "reload and reapply" affordance or
a way to view the conflict.

steps.action: User A: reload. Reapply A's edit. Save again.
steps.expected: Save succeeds against the fresh version.

expected_overall: Optimistic locking is in place; stale saves are visible.
pass_criteria:
Stale save was rejected AND the message explained why AND a
recovery path was offered.
why_this_matters:
Without optimistic locking, the easiest concurrency bug — User A
loads, User B saves, User A saves over B — destroys data silently.

title: A late posting racing the period close lands on the correct side
goal:
Verify that when a Controller initiates period close at the same
moment another user is posting a transaction dated in that period,
exactly one outcome occurs: either the transaction posts and is
included in the closed period, or the close completes and the
transaction is rejected as backdated. The transaction never
silently lands in a closed period.
preconditions: A Controller user (User A) is in a position to close the period.
A second user (User B) has a draft transaction (e.g., a customer invoice or vendor invoice) ready to post, dated within the period being closed.

steps.action:
User B: open the draft transaction in their session. Do not
submit yet.
steps.expected: Draft is editable.

steps.action:
User A: initiate period close. Just before final confirmation,
pause (e.g., review the close-summary screen).
steps.expected: Close is staged but not yet committed.

steps.action: User B: submit / post their transaction.
steps.expected:
Outcome A — transaction posts before close commits: the
transaction is included in the period. Close, when it commits,
sees this transaction.
Outcome B — close commits before the transaction posts: the
transaction is rejected with a clear "period closed" message.

steps.action: User A: complete the close.
steps.expected: Period is closed.

steps.action: Open reports for the closed period.
steps.expected:
If outcome A applied, the transaction appears in the period's
reports. If outcome B applied, it does not. There is no third
outcome.

expected_overall: No transaction lands in a closed period silently.
pass_criteria:
Either outcome A or outcome B occurred AND there is no
inconsistency between the report and the transaction status.

title: Two simultaneous reservations against the same on-hand cannot exceed available
goal:
Verify that when two users try to reserve / allocate inventory
against the same on-hand quantity, the system serializes the
reservations and the second user gets a clear "insufficient stock"
message — never allowing the system to oversell.
preconditions: One part has on-hand quantity of exactly 10.
Two users with reservation authority exist.

steps.action:
User A: open a draft sales order for the part. Set the quantity
to 8.
steps.expected: Draft accepts. Available is shown as 10.

steps.action:
User B: in a separate session, open another draft order for the
same part. Set quantity to 5.
steps.expected:
Draft accepts. Available is shown as 10 (or, if B opened after
A reserved, 2 — depends on whether A has confirmed yet).

steps.action: User A: confirm / reserve.
steps.expected: Reservation succeeds. On-hand or available drops by 8.

steps.action: User B: confirm / reserve their order for 5.
steps.expected:
Either: (a) the reservation is rejected with a clear message
that only 2 are available; or (b) the order is split so 2 are
reserved and 3 are backordered. Silently reserving 5 (over-
committing) is the failure mode.

steps.action:
Open the inventory record. Verify that total reserved cannot
exceed total on-hand.
steps.expected: Reserved ≤ on-hand always.

expected_overall: Simultaneous reservations cannot oversell.
pass_criteria:
Reserved quantity never exceeds on-hand AND the second user got a
clear conflict outcome.
why_this_matters:
Reservation races are the textbook concurrency bug. An ERP that
oversells silently is responsible for missed customer commitments
no one anticipated.

title: Two operators scanning the same WO start barcode produces one started entry
goal:
Verify that two Floor Operators who scan the start barcode for the
same WO operation at the same moment do not both end up with open
labor entries against the same op. Either one is started and the
other gets a clear "already started" message, or the system pairs
both as collaborators on the same op (only if it explicitly
supports multi-operator).
preconditions: Two Floor Operator users exist.
At least one released WO with a Released operation exists.

steps.action:
Operator A and Operator B simultaneously scan the WO start
barcode for the same operation.
steps.expected:
One of:
(a) One scan starts the operation; the other returns a clear
    message ("operation already started by Operator A") and
    offers to join as a collaborator if applicable.
(b) Both scans are accepted only if the system explicitly
    supports multi-operator labor on one operation.

What is NOT acceptable: both scans appear to succeed but only
one labor record exists, with no indication to the second
operator that they were silently dropped.

steps.action: Open the WO operation's labor records.
steps.expected:
Either one labor record (with a clear actor) or two if the
system supports it. No orphaned or duplicated records.

expected_overall: Concurrent WO starts are serialized correctly.
pass_criteria:
No orphaned labor record AND no silent loss of one operator's
start.

title: Attach a file to a record and verify retrieval
goal:
Verify arbitrary files (PDFs, images, drawings) can be attached
to records (PO, customer, part, WO), are scoped to the record,
retrievable later, and respect any storage / size limits with
clear messages.
preconditions: At least one part record exists.

steps.action: Open a part record. Attach a PDF drawing (e.g., 2 MB).
steps.expected:
Upload completes. File is listed under attachments with
filename, size, uploaded-by, timestamp.

steps.action: Reload the record. Re-download the file.
steps.expected: File downloads identically (matching size; identical content).

steps.action:
Try to upload an oversized file (e.g., over the documented
limit).
steps.expected:
Upload is rejected with a clear message about the size limit.
Other attachments are unaffected.

steps.action:
Open a different record (e.g., a different part) and verify
the attachment is NOT visible there.
steps.expected: Attachment is scoped to its record only.

expected_overall: Attachments persist and are correctly scoped.
pass_criteria:
File uploaded AND re-downloaded intact AND oversized clearly
rejected AND scoping respected.

title: Generate a bill of lading (BOL) for a shipment
goal:
Verify a BOL generates for a shipment with shipper, consignee,
carrier, freight terms, weight, package count, hazmat block (if
applicable).
preconditions: A shipment ready to dispatch exists.
Carrier and weight info are entered.

steps.action: Open the shipment. Generate the BOL.
steps.expected:
BOL PDF includes shipper, consignee, carrier, freight terms,
package count, total weight.

steps.action: For a hazmat-flagged part, generate the BOL.
steps.expected: Hazmat declaration block is present (UN number, hazard class).

expected_overall: BOL generation is correct for both standard and hazmat shipments.
pass_criteria: Standard BOL has required fields AND hazmat BOL adds hazmat fields.

title: Customer invoice emails to the customer with PDF attached
goal:
Verify a posted customer invoice can be emailed to the customer's
AR contact, the email body uses a configurable template, the PDF
is attached, and delivery succeeds.
preconditions: At least one posted customer invoice exists.
The customer has a valid AR email address.

steps.action: Open the invoice. Use the email action.
steps.expected:
Email composer opens with default template, recipient = AR
contact, subject including invoice number, PDF attached.

steps.action: Send.
steps.expected:
Email is dispatched. The send is logged on the invoice (date,
recipient, status).

steps.action: Check the recipient mailbox (test address).
steps.expected: Email arrives. PDF opens correctly.

expected_overall: Invoice email delivery works end-to-end.
pass_criteria:
Email sent AND received AND PDF intact AND send is logged on the
invoice.

title: Generate a packing slip from a packed shipment
goal:
Verify the packing slip generates with: customer info, customer
PO, ship-to address, line items with quantities, any special
handling notes, no pricing.
preconditions: A packed (but possibly not yet shipped) order exists.

steps.action: Open the packed order. Generate the packing slip PDF.
steps.expected:
Packing slip generates with the listed fields. Pricing is
omitted by design.

steps.action:
Verify the PDF prints correctly to a standard printer (or
preview matches a print-ready layout).
steps.expected: Print preview is correct.

expected_overall: Packing slip is generation-ready.
pass_criteria: Slip generates AND has all needed fields AND no pricing.

title: Generate a clean PO PDF including all standard fields
goal:
Verify the PO PDF generates with full header (company logo,
vendor, PO number, terms, ship-to), line items, totals, and any
attached terms-and-conditions.
preconditions: At least one issued PO exists.

steps.action: Open an issued PO. Generate the PDF.
steps.expected:
PDF downloads. It includes: company info / logo, vendor address,
PO number, line items with quantities, prices, totals, payment
terms.

steps.action:
Open the PDF in a viewer. Verify legibility, no overlapping
text, no broken layout, no truncated long item descriptions.
steps.expected: Layout is correct.

expected_overall: PO PDF is usable as a real vendor-facing document.
pass_criteria: PDF generates AND fields complete AND no layout issues.

title: Generate a customer statement showing open invoices and credits
goal:
Verify a customer statement can be generated for a date range
showing all open invoices, payments, and credits with running
balance, and emailed to the customer.
preconditions: At least one customer with multiple invoices and at least one payment exists.

steps.action:
Open the statement area. Select customer ACME and a date range
covering recent activity.
steps.expected:
Statement shows opening balance, transactions in range, closing
balance, with each invoice / payment / credit line.

steps.action: Email the statement to the customer's AR contact.
steps.expected: Email sends. Statement is logged on the customer record.

expected_overall: Customer statement is correct and deliverable.
pass_criteria: Statement balance matches AR aging AND email sent AND log recorded.

title: A labor entry that spans a DST transition records correct elapsed time
goal:
Verify that an operator who clocks in before a daylight-savings time
shift and clocks out after it gets credit for the actual elapsed
hours — not one hour more (fall-back) and not one hour less
(spring-forward).
preconditions: The tenant is configured to a time zone that observes DST (e.g., Pacific from P0-TENANT-003).
At least one Floor Operator user is set up with WO access.
notes:
This case requires the system clock to actually pass through a DST
boundary OR the application to support backdating a labor entry
that crosses a known DST transition. If neither is possible in the
test environment, mark the case Blocked and report the limitation.

steps.action:
Identify a known historical DST transition (e.g., the most
recent fall-back). Backdate-create a labor entry for an operator
that starts 30 minutes before the transition and ends 30 minutes
after, in local clock time.
steps.expected: Entry accepts the start and end timestamps.

steps.action: Read the recorded elapsed time on the labor entry.
steps.expected:
Elapsed time is 60 minutes, NOT 120 minutes (fall-back) or 0
(spring-forward).

steps.action:
Run the labor distribution report. The operator's time for that
day reflects 60 minutes, not 120.
steps.expected: Match.

expected_overall: DST does not corrupt elapsed-time calculations.
pass_criteria:
Recorded elapsed time matches actual elapsed minutes regardless of
the DST transition.
why_this_matters:
Twice a year, every labor system that gets this wrong over- or
under-pays employees. The bug is invisible until payroll runs.

title: Transactions dated on the last and first days of the fiscal year post to the correct year
goal:
Verify that transactions dated December 31 fall in the closing year
and transactions dated January 1 fall in the new year, both for
reporting and for the Year-To-Date calculations on every report.
preconditions: The fiscal year starts January 1 (per P0-TENANT-004).
At least two open periods straddling the year-end boundary exist.

steps.action:
Post a journal entry dated December 31 (last day of fiscal year)
to a revenue account, $100.
steps.expected: Entry posts in the December period.

steps.action:
Post a journal entry dated January 1 (first day of next fiscal
year) to the same account, $100.
steps.expected: Entry posts in the January period of the new fiscal year.

steps.action: Run the P&L for the closing year.
steps.expected:
Includes the December 31 entry; does NOT include the January 1
entry.

steps.action: Run the P&L for the new year, year-to-date as of January 1.
steps.expected:
Includes the January 1 entry; does NOT include the December 31
entry.

expected_overall:
The fiscal-year boundary is honored cleanly. No transaction lands
in the wrong year.
pass_criteria:
Each transaction appears in exactly one year's P&L AND not in the
other.
why_this_matters:
Off-by-one on the year boundary mis-states annual results and
triggers tax issues. This is among the highest-stakes edge-case
bugs.

title: Transactions dated February 29 in a leap year post correctly
goal:
Verify that February 29 is accepted as a transaction date in a leap
year and rejected in a non-leap year, that aging from February 29
computes correctly, and that recurring entries scheduled for "the
29th of every month" handle non-February months.
preconditions: The tenant date is set to (or backdated to) a known leap year (e.g., 2028 or use a historical leap year if backdating works).
A test customer exists.

steps.action: Post an AR opening balance dated February 29 in a leap year.
steps.expected: Date accepts. Entry posts.

steps.action:
In a non-leap year context (e.g., advance the test date to
February 29 of a non-leap year), try to enter a transaction with
the same date.
steps.expected: Date is rejected with a clear "not a valid date" message.

steps.action:
Run AR aging on March 30 of the leap year. The Feb 29 invoice
should be 30 days old.
steps.expected: Age computed correctly (March 30 - February 29 = 30 days).

steps.action:
If the system supports recurring JEs scheduled for "29th
monthly" (P5-JE-002), set one up and verify how it handles
March, April, etc.
steps.expected:
Either rolls to the 28th in non-29-bearing months OR rolls to
the last day of the month — and the choice is documented in the
schedule UI. Silent skipping is unacceptable.

expected_overall:
February 29 is valid in a leap year, invalid otherwise; aging and
recurring schedules both handle the boundary cleanly.
pass_criteria:
Leap-year date accepted AND non-leap-year date rejected AND aging
correct AND recurring rule disposition is explicit.

title: Transaction posted near midnight uses the tenant time zone, not UTC
goal:
Verify that a transaction posted at, e.g., 11:50 PM tenant-local
time on day X stays on day X in reports — not day X+1 because the
server stored it as UTC.
preconditions: The tenant is configured to a non-UTC time zone (Pacific from P0-TENANT-003 is fine).
It is currently late evening tenant-local time, OR the test environment supports backdating a timestamp to one.

steps.action:
At 11:50 PM tenant-local time (or backdate-create a transaction
with that local timestamp), post a journal entry.
steps.expected:
Entry posts. The visible posting timestamp shows 11:50 PM tenant
local time.

steps.action: Run a daily transaction register filtered to "today" tenant-time.
steps.expected:
The 11:50 PM entry is included in today's register, not
tomorrow's.

steps.action: Run the same register filtered to "tomorrow" tenant-time.
steps.expected: The 11:50 PM entry is NOT included.

expected_overall: Tenant time zone determines the calendar day for transactions.
pass_criteria:
Late-evening tenant-local entry appears under the correct tenant
date in both daily filters.
why_this_matters:
When servers store UTC and clients display local, "today" can mean
two different days depending on which side resolved it. The
invariant is "transaction date is what the user thought it was when
they clicked save." Anything else is a bug.

title: Transactions preserve documented decimal precision for quantities and costs
goal:
Verify that quantities and unit costs are stored at the precision
the application documents (typically four decimals on quantity, six
on unit cost) and that aggregations don't round prematurely.
preconditions: At least one part with a non-trivial UoM (e.g., RM-STEEL-1018-3X3 in feet).

steps.action:
On a draft PO, enter a quantity of 100.1234 ft at unit cost
$4.567890. Save.
steps.expected: Form accepts the precision. Saved values match exactly.

steps.action:
Reopen the line. Verify both values are preserved at full
precision.
steps.expected:
Saved quantity = 100.1234, saved unit cost = 4.567890. No
truncation.

steps.action:
Compute the line extension by hand: 100.1234 × 4.567890 =
$457.351... Compare to the system's line total (rounded per
the application's documented rule, typically $457.35).
steps.expected:
Match within the documented rounding rule. The rule itself is
visible somewhere (label, tooltip, or doc), not silent.

steps.action:
Receive the line at the same quantity and verify the inventory
valuation extension equals the same line extension.
steps.expected: Match.

expected_overall:
Decimal precision is preserved through entry, save, reload, and
derived calculations. Rounding is explicit.
pass_criteria:
Stored values match input AND derived line equals the hand
computation within the documented rounding rule.
why_this_matters:
Silent precision loss compounds. A part priced at $0.123456 per
unit loses real money on six-figure orders if the system rounds
early.

title: A rate movement between invoice date and payment date posts a realized FX gain or loss
goal:
Verify that an invoice in foreign currency settled at a different
FX rate than it was issued posts a realized gain or loss to the
correct GL account — not a silent home-currency adjustment buried
in revenue.
preconditions: A foreign-currency customer exists.
An open invoice in that currency exists, posted at one rate.
The FX rate has been updated to a different rate before payment.

steps.action:
Read the invoice's home-currency value at issuance (rate × foreign
amount).
steps.expected: Home-currency value visible.

steps.action: Apply a payment in the foreign currency at the new rate.
steps.expected:
Payment posts. Home-currency cash receipt = foreign amount × new
rate.

steps.action: Examine the JE postings.
steps.expected:
The difference between the invoice's home value and the
payment's home value posts to a Realized FX Gain (or Loss)
account — not to revenue, not to AR.

expected_overall:
Rate movement is captured as a realized FX gain or loss in the
right account.
pass_criteria:
Gain or loss equals (foreign amount × (new rate - old rate)) within
$0.01 AND posts to the FX gain/loss account.

title: Multi-currency conversion rounds consistently across header, lines, and totals
goal:
Verify that a transaction in a foreign currency, when displayed or
posted in the home currency, has line totals that sum to the header
total within the application's documented rounding tolerance — and
that the GL entries balance to zero in both currencies.
preconditions: At least one foreign-currency vendor or customer exists (P2-VENDOR-003 or P2-CUST-004).
An FX rate is configured for the relevant pair.

steps.action:
Create a vendor invoice in EUR with three line items at non-round
amounts (e.g., €33.33, €33.33, €33.34). Post.
steps.expected: Header total in EUR: €100.00. Posts cleanly.

steps.action:
Read the home-currency (USD) values on the invoice header and
lines.
steps.expected:
The sum of the line USD values equals the header USD value
within $0.01 (or whatever tolerance the application documents).
The tolerance is explicitly stated, not silent.

steps.action: Open the GL postings.
steps.expected:
Postings balance to zero in both EUR (transaction currency) and
USD (home currency). No rounding remainder is left dangling.

expected_overall:
FX-converted transactions sum cleanly and post a balanced ledger
in both currencies.
pass_criteria:
Line USD totals match header USD total within tolerance AND GL is
balanced in both currencies.
why_this_matters:
Penny rounding errors in FX accumulate — the kind of bug that
shows up as a $0.03 imbalance after a thousand multi-currency
invoices and takes a week to track down.

title: A large data export (5,000+ rows) downloads without truncation or timeout
goal:
Verify a bulk export from a list view (e.g., parts CSV / Excel)
completes for a 5,000-row dataset and the file contains every row
represented in the source view.
preconditions: 5,000+ records exist for the entity being exported.

steps.action:
Open the parts list. Trigger an export to CSV (or Excel) of all
rows.
steps.expected:
Export starts. The application either streams the file or shows
progress / completion notice rather than appearing to hang.

steps.action: Wait for completion. Download the file. Open it.
steps.expected:
File opens. Row count (excluding header) equals the list view's
total count.

steps.action:
Spot-check a row from the middle and a row from the end of the
file against the source.
steps.expected: Both rows match the source. No silent truncation.

expected_overall: Bulk export is faithful and complete at scale.
pass_criteria: File row count equals source count AND spot-checked rows match.
why_this_matters:
Exports that silently truncate at, say, 1,000 rows are common —
and the user usually only finds out when their report's totals
are wrong.

title: A list view remains usable when the underlying record count crosses 5,000
goal:
Verify that a list view (e.g., parts) loads, filters, sorts, and
paginates within tolerable response time when the record count is
in the thousands. Many ERPs are usable at 100 rows and useless at
10,000.
preconditions:
Either 5,000+ part records exist in the test environment, OR a bulk-seed
script can be run to create them.
notes:
If 5,000 records cannot be seeded, mark the case Blocked and report
it. Do not weaken the case to fit a smaller dataset.

steps.action: Open the parts list view.
steps.expected:
First page of results renders within 5 seconds. Total count is
visible (e.g., "5,234 parts").

steps.action:
Apply a filter (e.g., "type = Raw material"). Type into the
filter — observe the filter behavior.
steps.expected: Filtered list returns within 3 seconds. Results count updates.

steps.action: Sort by a non-indexed-looking column (e.g., description).
steps.expected: Sort completes within 5 seconds. Pages reflect sorted order.

steps.action: Paginate to the last page.
steps.expected:
Last page loads within 5 seconds. Returns the expected tail of
the sorted set.

steps.action: Use the search box for a specific part number.
steps.expected: Search resolves within 2 seconds.

expected_overall: Large list view stays responsive within the listed time bounds.
pass_criteria:
Each operation completes within its documented time bound AND no
partial / truncated results are silently returned.
why_this_matters:
ERP list views are where everyday users live. A list that's fast
at 100 records and unusable at 5,000 silently destroys productivity
as the company grows.

title: Tax rate changes mid-period; each transaction taxes per its own date
goal:
Verify that when a tax rate changes effective on a date mid-period,
invoices dated before the change use the old rate and invoices
dated after use the new rate — and that the rate-change effective
date is itself audited.
preconditions: At least one tax code with a known rate exists (P1-TAX-001).
The current period is open and at least 5 days have passed.

steps.action:
Update the tax code: change the rate, with an effective date
mid-period (e.g., the 15th).
steps.expected:
The application requires an effective date for the change. Both
the old and new rates remain visible with their effective ranges.

steps.action:
Create a customer invoice dated the 14th of the period (before
the change).
steps.expected: Tax computes using the old rate.

steps.action:
Create a customer invoice dated the 16th of the period (after
the change).
steps.expected: Tax computes using the new rate.

steps.action: Open the tax code's audit log.
steps.expected:
The rate change is recorded with actor, timestamp, prior rate,
new rate, and effective date.

expected_overall:
Tax rate change is dated and per-transaction; history of the rate
is preserved.
pass_criteria:
Old-rate invoice taxes with old rate AND new-rate invoice taxes
with new rate AND audit captures the change cleanly.
why_this_matters:
ERPs that just overwrite a tax rate and apply it retroactively
invalidate every prior invoice's tax line. Tax-rate change has to
be effective-dated, not destructive.

title: Posted financials sync to the connected accounting system
goal:
Verify that with the accounting integration set to "connected"
(P0-INTEG-001), each posted financial transaction (invoice, JE,
payment) creates a corresponding record in the external accounting
system within the documented sync window.
preconditions: The accounting integration is configured to a test sandbox (not real).
At least one customer invoice has been posted in the period.

steps.action:
Post a customer invoice (or use one already posted). Open the
sync status / queue.
steps.expected: The invoice appears in the queue, status "Pending sync."

steps.action: Wait for the next sync (or trigger manually).
steps.expected:
Invoice transitions to "Synced." Sync log captures the external
ID.

steps.action:
Open the external accounting sandbox. Find the corresponding
record.
steps.expected:
Record exists with matching customer, amount, date, and
reference.

steps.action:
Force a sync failure (e.g., temporarily revoke the credential).
Post another transaction.
steps.expected:
Failure is captured in the sync log with a clear reason. The
transaction stays "Pending" or "Failed" but is NOT silently
dropped.

expected_overall: Outbound accounting sync is reliable and surfaces failures.
pass_criteria:
Successful sync captures external ID AND failure is visible AND
no silent loss.

title: Outbound EDI 810 invoice dispatched to a customer
goal:
Verify that posting a customer invoice to an EDI customer
generates and sends an 810 with line / pricing / tax / terms
matching the posted invoice.
preconditions: A trading partner expects 810.
A posted customer invoice for that customer exists.

steps.action: Post an invoice to an EDI-configured customer.
steps.expected: 810 is generated and queued / dispatched.

steps.action:
Inspect the 810. Verify lines, totals, taxes, terms, customer
PO, ship-to / bill-to all match the posted invoice.
steps.expected: Match.

steps.action:
Track delivery / acknowledgment from the trading partner (a
997 functional ack).
steps.expected:
997 is received and logged. The invoice's EDI status updates
to "Acknowledged."

expected_overall: 810 dispatch and acknowledgment loop work end-to-end.
pass_criteria:
810 dispatched AND fields tie to invoice AND 997 acknowledged
status visible.

title: Inbound EDI 850 from a customer creates a sales order
goal:
Verify the application can ingest an EDI 850 (PO from a customer)
and create a corresponding sales order with correct customer,
line items, prices, and ship-to / bill-to.
preconditions: The application supports EDI inbound (or has a test trading partner configured).
At least one customer is set up for EDI.

steps.action:
Submit a sample EDI 850 file (with customer / part numbers
mapped per the trading partner's specification).
steps.expected:
File is ingested. Sales order is created in draft (or open)
status.

steps.action:
Open the SO. Verify customer, lines, prices, ship-to, customer
PO, and date all came through.
steps.expected: All fields match the 850.

steps.action: Submit a malformed 850 (bad segment).
steps.expected:
File is rejected. Error is logged. SO is NOT created. The user
can see what's wrong.

expected_overall:
EDI 850 inbound creates SOs cleanly with error visibility on
malformed input.
pass_criteria:
Valid 850 → SO with matching fields AND malformed 850 produces a
clear error.

title: Outbound EDI 855 acknowledges an incoming customer PO
goal:
Verify that after ingesting an 850 (or accepting a customer PO),
the application generates and dispatches a corresponding 855
(PO acknowledgment) with the right approval / change codes.
preconditions: At least one inbound 850 has produced an SO (per INTEG-EDI-850-001).

steps.action: Confirm or accept the SO derived from an inbound 850.
steps.expected: An 855 generation action is available (or auto-fires).

steps.action: Generate / send the 855.
steps.expected:
855 is dispatched to the trading partner. The application logs
the send.

steps.action: Modify a line price on the SO. Re-send 855.
steps.expected:
Updated 855 reflects the price change with the appropriate
change code, NOT silently confirming the original.

expected_overall: 855 reflects the SO's actual state with proper change codes.
pass_criteria:
855 dispatched AND fields match SO AND change reflected on
resend.

title: Outbound EDI 856 ASN dispatched at shipment
goal:
Verify that when a shipment goes out (P4-SHIP), the application
generates and sends an 856 ASN to the customer with the correct
packing structure (pack-level, item-level, serial-level if
applicable).
preconditions: A trading partner expects 856.
A shipment is ready to send.

steps.action: Ship a packed order to an EDI customer.
steps.expected:
856 is generated. The application's send log shows the
dispatch.

steps.action: Inspect the generated 856 (raw or via a test partner).
steps.expected:
Pack hierarchy is correct (shipment → packs → cartons →
items). Tracking number, ship date, and ship-to are present.

steps.action: For a serialized product, verify the 856 includes serials.
steps.expected: Serials present at item level.

expected_overall:
856 dispatch is correct for both serialized and non-serialized
products.
pass_criteria:
856 generated AND fields complete AND serials included where
applicable.

title: Export a list to CSV / Excel and re-import to verify the round-trip
goal:
Verify that exporting an entity list to CSV / Excel produces a file
that can be re-imported (after appropriate edits) without breaking
references or losing data fidelity.
preconditions: At least 20 records of one entity exist.

steps.action: Open the entity list. Export all to CSV.
steps.expected: File downloads. Header row matches the import template.

steps.action:
Open the file. Verify the row count equals the source count
(no truncation).
steps.expected: Match.

steps.action: Edit a non-key field on 5 rows. Save. Re-import.
steps.expected:
Re-import detects the rows as updates (not duplicates),
updates the modified field, and reports the update count.

expected_overall: File round-trips cleanly.
pass_criteria:
Exported = source AND re-import updates correctly AND no
duplicates created.

title: Generic file-based import works with row-level error reporting
goal:
Verify file-based import for any importable entity (parts,
vendors, customers, GL accounts) works with documented templates,
flags row errors with line and column detail, and never silently
drops bad rows or partial outcomes.
preconditions: At least one importable entity has a documented CSV / Excel template.

steps.action:
Pick an importable entity. Download its template. Fill in 10
rows: 8 valid, 2 with errors of different types (e.g., missing
required field, invalid foreign key, duplicate key).
steps.expected: Template is clear about required vs. optional fields.

steps.action: Upload the file.
steps.expected:
Validation summary shows 8 rows ready, 2 errored. Each error
gives line number, column, and reason.

steps.action: Confirm import.
steps.expected:
8 records created. The 2 bad rows are NOT silently skipped —
they appear in an error report the user can fix and re-upload.

expected_overall: File-based import works with row-level error visibility.
pass_criteria:
Valid rows imported AND bad rows reported AND no silent partial
outcomes.

title: Customer list supports search, filter, sort, and pagination
goal:
Verify the customer list view: partial-match search by name and ID,
filter by status / region / credit band, sort on every column,
and pagination over a non-trivial dataset.
preconditions: At least 50 customers exist (for filter / sort / paginate coverage). Some active, some inactive.

steps.action:
Open the customer list. Type a partial customer name (e.g.,
"ACM") into search.
steps.expected:
Results filter live to matching customers (case-insensitive,
partial match).

steps.action: Apply a filter: status = Inactive.
steps.expected: Only inactive customers shown.

steps.action: Sort by name (ascending), then by created date (descending).
steps.expected: Sort applied. Results reflect sort order.

steps.action: Paginate to last page.
steps.expected: Last page returns the expected tail of the sorted set.

steps.action: Save the current filter / sort as a named view (if supported).
steps.expected: Saved view appears in the list. Re-opening it restores filters.

expected_overall: Customer list is fully usable for normal-day workflows.
pass_criteria: Search / filter / sort / paginate / saved view all work correctly.

title: Invoice list supports filter by customer, status, aging, and sort
goal:
Verify the customer invoice list supports filter by customer,
status (draft / sent / paid / partial / void), aging bucket, and
sort on every column.
preconditions: At least 20 customer invoices exist across statuses and aging buckets.

steps.action:
Open the invoice list. Filter to status = Open + aging > 30
days.
steps.expected: Result matches.

steps.action: Sort by amount due (descending).
steps.expected: Sort applied.

steps.action: Search by customer PO number.
steps.expected: Match works.

expected_overall: Invoice list is usable for AR / collections workflows.
pass_criteria: Filters / sort / search all work correctly.

title: Part list supports search, filter (type, lot/serial, status), sort, paginate
goal:
Verify the part list view supports search by part number /
description, filter by type (raw / WIP / FG / service), filter by
tracking (lot / serial / none), sort on every column, paginate.
preconditions: At least 50 parts exist across multiple types and tracking modes.

steps.action: Open the parts list. Search for partial part number.
steps.expected: Partial match works on both part number and description.

steps.action: Filter to type = Raw material AND tracking = Lot.
steps.expected: Result is the intersection.

steps.action: Sort by description (ascending), then by primary UoM.
steps.expected: Sort applied.

steps.action: Paginate to last page.
steps.expected: Last page returns expected tail.

expected_overall: Part list is usable for normal engineering / procurement workflows.
pass_criteria: Search / filter / sort / paginate all work correctly.

title: PO list supports filter by vendor, status, date range, and sort
goal:
Verify the PO list view supports filtering by vendor, status
(draft / approved / issued / partially received / received / closed),
date range, and sorting.
preconditions: At least 20 POs exist across statuses and vendors.

steps.action:
Open the PO list. Filter to vendor = Pacific Steel Supply +
status = Issued.
steps.expected: Result matches.

steps.action: Add a date range: issued in last 30 days.
steps.expected: Result narrows.

steps.action: Sort by total amount (descending).
steps.expected: Largest POs first.

expected_overall: PO list is usable for procurement workflows.
pass_criteria: Filters / sort all work correctly.

title: Vendor list supports search, filter (1099, currency), sort, and paginate
goal:
Verify the vendor list view supports the same canonical UX
(search / filter / sort / paginate) and adds vendor-specific
filters: 1099 eligibility, currency.
preconditions: At least 20 vendors exist with mixed 1099 status and at least two currencies.

steps.action: Open the vendor list. Filter to 1099-eligible only.
steps.expected: Result is the vendors flagged 1099-eligible.

steps.action: Add a second filter: currency = JPY.
steps.expected: Result is the intersection.

steps.action: Sort by total YTD spend (descending) if available.
steps.expected: Sort applied.

steps.action:
Search for partial vendor name. Verify case-insensitive partial
match.
steps.expected: Partial match works.

expected_overall: Vendor list supports vendor-specific filters cleanly.
pass_criteria: All filters / sort / search behave correctly.

title: WO list supports filter by status, work center, due date, and sort
goal:
Verify the WO list view filters by status (planned / released /
in-progress / complete / closed), by work center, by due date
range, and sorts on every column including computed (% complete,
variance).
preconditions: At least 30 WOs exist across multiple statuses.

steps.action:
Open the WO list. Filter to "in-progress" + work center =
Press Shop.
steps.expected: Result matches both filters.

steps.action: Add a date range: due in next 7 days.
steps.expected: Result narrows.

steps.action: Sort by due date (ascending).
steps.expected: Earliest due first.

steps.action: Search by WO number (partial).
steps.expected: Partial match works.

expected_overall: WO list is usable for daily floor management.
pass_criteria: Filters / sort / search all work correctly.

title: Pending approval that's been waiting beyond a threshold reminds the approver
goal:
Verify that a pending approval (e.g., requisition, PO, ECR, JE)
that's been waiting beyond a documented threshold fires a reminder
to the approver.
preconditions: At least one approval workflow exists with a documented reminder threshold (e.g., 24 hours).

steps.action:
Submit a requisition (or ECR / JE) requiring approval. Wait
past the threshold (or backdate the submission).
steps.expected: A reminder notification fires for the approver.

steps.action: Approve or reject.
steps.expected: Reminder clears.

expected_overall: Approval reminders nudge approvers without spamming them.
pass_criteria: Reminder fires past threshold AND clears on action AND not duplicated.

title: AR aging triggers fire at documented aging buckets
goal:
Verify that when an invoice crosses an aging bucket boundary
(e.g., enters 31-60 days past due), a notification fires to the
collections / controller team, surfacing the invoice for action.
preconditions: At least one customer invoice exists.

steps.action:
Backdate (or wait) so an existing invoice crosses into the
31-60 day past-due bucket.
steps.expected: An AR aging notification fires.

steps.action: Apply payment to the invoice.
steps.expected: Alert clears.

expected_overall: Aging-bucket alert fires and clears.
pass_criteria: Alert fires at bucket transition AND clears on payment.

title: Low-stock alert fires when on-hand drops below reorder point
goal:
Verify that when on-hand for a part drops below its reorder point,
a notification fires to the configured recipient(s), and that the
notification clears once stock is replenished above the point.
preconditions: A part has reorder point and notification subscribers configured (P3-SS-001).

steps.action: Issue or adjust on-hand to drop below the reorder point.
steps.expected:
A low-stock notification fires within the documented window
(in-app and / or email per channel config).

steps.action: Receive new stock to bring on-hand back above reorder point.
steps.expected: The active alert clears (or a "resolved" indicator appears).

steps.action:
Repeat the cycle to verify it doesn't fire-and-stick after
multiple cycles.
steps.expected: Each crossing fires fresh; resolved alerts don't pile up.

expected_overall: Low-stock alert fires and clears predictably.
pass_criteria:
Alert fires on threshold breach AND clears on recovery AND no
duplicate / stuck alerts.

title: PM that's past its scheduled date triggers an overdue alert
goal:
Verify a scheduled PM that has not been started by its scheduled
date fires an alert to the maintenance manager and asset owner.
preconditions: A PM is scheduled with a date in the past (or backdate to make it past due).

steps.action: Configure or backdate a PM to be past its scheduled start.
steps.expected: An overdue alert fires for the maintenance manager.

steps.action: Start the PM.
steps.expected: Alert clears.

expected_overall: Overdue PM alert fires and clears.
pass_criteria: Alert fires when overdue AND clears on PM start.

title: PO past expected delivery date triggers an overdue alert
goal:
Verify that an issued PO whose expected delivery date has passed
without full receipt fires an overdue alert to the buyer and / or
buyer team.
preconditions: At least one issued PO with an expected delivery date in the past and not fully received.

steps.action:
Issue (or backdate) a PO so its expected delivery date is in
the past with no receipts.
steps.expected: An overdue notification fires for the buyer.

steps.action: Receive the PO fully.
steps.expected: Alert clears.

steps.action: Open the PO. Verify the alert history is auditable.
steps.expected: History shows the alert fired and resolved.

expected_overall: Overdue PO alert fires and clears.
pass_criteria:
Alert present after due date AND cleared on receipt AND history
recorded.

title: Create the first administrator account
goal:
Establish the first user. This person will configure the rest of
the company.
preconditions: You completed P0-INSTALL-001.
No user accounts exist yet.

steps.action:
From the starting page, follow the path that begins setup or
creates the first account.
steps.expected:
A form appears asking for information about the person setting
up the company. At minimum: name, email address, and a password
(or equivalent way to secure the account).

steps.action:
Fill in the form with these values:
  Name: Alex Morgan
  Email: alex.morgan@cascade-components.example
  Password: a strong password of your choice (write it down —
  you will need it)
Submit the form.
steps.expected:
The form accepts the submission. You are either signed in
automatically or directed to sign in with the credentials you
just created.

steps.action:
If you were not automatically signed in, sign in using the email
and password from step 2.
steps.expected:
You are signed in as Alex Morgan. The application shows it
recognizes you (your name, initials, or email is visible
somewhere on the page).

expected_overall:
An administrator account exists and is signed in. The system
recognizes this person as the first user.
pass_criteria:
After completing all steps, you are signed in to the application
with the account you just created.
why_this_matters:
This is the moment the company becomes a real tenant in the
application. Anything that goes wrong here blocks all further setup.

negative_variants.title: Reject obviously invalid email
negative_variants.action: In the email field, enter "not-an-email" and try to submit.
negative_variants.expected:
The form blocks submission. A message in plain English explains
that the email address is not valid. The message does not say
anything like "regex match failed" or "validation error code 7."
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Submission was blocked AND the message was understandable to a
non-technical person.

negative_variants.title: Reject empty required fields
negative_variants.action: Leave one of the required fields empty and try to submit.
negative_variants.expected:
The form blocks submission. The message identifies which field
is missing, in plain language.
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Submission was blocked AND the missing field was clearly
identified.

negative_variants.title: Reject weak password (if password rules apply)
negative_variants.action: Try to submit with the password "password" or "12345."
negative_variants.expected:
Either the form blocks submission with a clear explanation of
what's wrong with the password, OR the application accepts it
but warns that the password is weak. Both behaviors are
acceptable; what is NOT acceptable is silently accepting "12345"
with no comment.
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Either the password was rejected with explanation, or accepted
with a visible warning. Silent acceptance fails this case.

title: Verify the first admin has the access needed to continue setup
goal:
Make sure the first admin's account has the permissions required to
perform the rest of Phase 0. A first admin who can sign in but
can't reach company settings is a critical bug.
preconditions: You completed P0-ADMIN-001 and are signed in as the first admin.

steps.action:
Look around the application's main navigation. Try to find areas
labeled like "Settings," "Setup," "Administration," "Company,"
"Configuration," or similar.
steps.expected: At least one such area is visible to you and you can open it.

steps.action:
Open whatever administrative area you found. Look at what's
there.
steps.expected:
The area contains options for company-level configuration:
things like company name, locale, currency, fiscal year, user
management, and so on. You may not understand every option yet,
but you can see them and they are not greyed out or marked
"permission denied."

expected_overall:
As the first admin, you can reach the configuration areas you'll
need for the rest of Phase 0.
pass_criteria:
You can open an administrative settings area AND the options inside
appear available to you (not blocked by permission errors).
why_this_matters:
Some applications bootstrap a first admin without granting them
full administrative permissions, leaving them unable to invite
other users or configure anything. That's an unacceptable bug at
this point in setup.

negative_variants.title: Sign-in URL bypasses admin gate
negative_variants.action:
Sign out, then attempt to reach an admin-only configuration page
by typing its URL directly while signed out.
negative_variants.expected:
You are redirected to sign-in. The admin page does not render
and no privileged data appears in the response.
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Direct URL access while signed out returns a sign-in prompt with
no privileged content leaked.

title: The admin audit log captures setup activity from Phase 0
goal:
Verify that the audit log is reachable and shows the consequential
admin actions performed during Phase 0 (tenant settings, role
creation, user invitation, integration choices).
preconditions: Phase 0 cases through P0-USER-003 have been run.

steps.action: Find and open the audit log area.
steps.expected: Audit log opens. Filtering by date and event type is available.

steps.action: Filter to the period covering this Phase 0 run.
steps.expected:
Entries are present for: company settings save, language change,
time zone change, fiscal year save, costing model save,
integration choices, role creation, user invitation, and any
sign-in / sign-out events.

steps.action: Open one entry — e.g., the costing model save.
steps.expected:
Detail shows actor, timestamp, prior value (none / unset), new
value (Standard Cost), and any reason / comment captured.

expected_overall: Phase 0 actions are visible in the audit log with full attribution.
pass_criteria:
Audit log is reachable AND lists the major Phase 0 actions AND
per-entry detail includes actor / before / after.

negative_variants.title: Audit log entries cannot be edited or deleted
negative_variants.action:
As an Administrator, attempt to edit or delete an entry in the
audit log.
negative_variants.expected:
The action is unavailable or blocked with a clear message that
audit entries are immutable.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Edit and delete are not possible from any UI surface.

negative_variants.title: Non-admin user cannot read the audit log
negative_variants.action:
Sign in as the Floor Operator from P0-USER-003 and try to
navigate to the audit log.
negative_variants.expected:
The audit log is hidden from navigation or returns a clear
"permission denied" response. No audit content leaks.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Audit log is inaccessible to non-privileged users.

title: Enroll the first admin in MFA and verify it's required on next sign-in
goal:
Configure multi-factor authentication on the first admin account
(the canonical "high-privilege account that absolutely needs MFA")
and verify subsequent sign-ins require the second factor.
preconditions: The first admin exists (P0-ADMIN-001).
The tenant has not yet enforced MFA on this account.

steps.action:
Sign in as the first admin. Find the security or account
settings area. Locate the option to enable MFA.
steps.expected:
An MFA enrollment option is available. Supported factors are
visible in plain language (authenticator app, SMS, hardware key).

steps.action:
Enroll an authenticator-app factor by scanning the displayed
QR code with any TOTP app. Enter the generated code to confirm.
steps.expected:
Enrollment completes. A confirmation appears. Backup recovery
codes are presented (and the user is prompted to save them).

steps.action: Sign out completely. Sign back in with the password.
steps.expected: A second-factor prompt appears before sign-in completes.

steps.action: Enter the current TOTP code. Confirm.
steps.expected: Sign-in completes.

steps.action:
Sign out again. Try to sign in with only the password (cancel
the MFA prompt).
steps.expected: Sign-in is blocked. No partial session is created.

expected_overall: MFA is enrolled and enforced. Password-only sign-in is rejected.
pass_criteria:
MFA enrollment succeeded AND backup codes were provided AND
password-only sign-in was blocked.
why_this_matters:
An ERP that doesn't support MFA on its highest-privilege accounts
is unsafe for any company that takes security seriously. Catching
this in P0 means it's set up correctly before there's anything
worth attacking.

negative_variants.title: Reject incorrect MFA code
negative_variants.action: During sign-in, enter a wrong 6-digit code.
negative_variants.expected:
Sign-in is rejected. A clear message says the code didn't match.
No partial session is created. After several wrong attempts,
either the user is rate-limited or the account is locked
(per P0-AUTH-003).
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Wrong code rejected AND no session granted AND repeated wrong
attempts trigger lockout / rate limit.

negative_variants.title: Reject expired TOTP code
negative_variants.action:
Capture a valid TOTP code, wait 90 seconds for it to expire,
then submit it.
negative_variants.expected:
Sign-in is rejected with a message indicating the code is
expired or invalid. No session is created.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Expired code is rejected AND no session granted.

negative_variants.title: Backup code is single-use
negative_variants.action:
Use one backup recovery code to sign in. Then attempt to use
the same backup code again on a subsequent sign-in.
negative_variants.expected:
The second use is rejected. The application makes clear the
code was already consumed.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Reused backup code is refused.

title: A user can reset their own password via email and sign back in
goal:
Verify the password-reset flow: user requests a reset, receives a
one-time link, sets a new password, and signs in with it. The old
password no longer works.
preconditions: At least two users exist with valid email addresses.

steps.action:
At the sign-in page, choose "Forgot password" (or equivalent).
Enter the email of the second user (Sam Rivera).
steps.expected:
The application confirms a reset link will be sent. The
message does NOT confirm or deny whether the email exists in
the system (account-enumeration protection).

steps.action: Open the user's mailbox. Open the reset email. Click the link.
steps.expected:
Reset form opens. The link should be limited-use and time-
bounded (typically 1 hour).

steps.action: Enter a new strong password. Confirm.
steps.expected: Password is updated. A confirmation email is sent to the user.

steps.action: Try to sign in with the OLD password.
steps.expected: Sign-in is rejected.

steps.action: Sign in with the NEW password.
steps.expected: Sign-in succeeds.

steps.action: Click the same reset link a second time.
steps.expected:
Link is rejected as already used (or expired). Reset form does
not open.

expected_overall: Password reset works end-to-end with single-use, time-bounded link.
pass_criteria:
New password works AND old password rejected AND reset link is
one-time-use.
why_this_matters:
Password reset via plain email is the most-attacked flow. Failure
modes — non-bounded links, multi-use tokens, account enumeration —
are all real-world security incidents.

negative_variants.title: Reset link expires after the documented window
negative_variants.action:
Request a reset, then wait beyond the documented link expiry
(or backdate as needed). Then click the link.
negative_variants.expected: Link is rejected as expired. A new reset can be requested.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Expired link blocks reset AND a fresh request still works.

negative_variants.title: Reset request for unknown email does not leak existence
negative_variants.action: Request a password reset for "no-such-user@example.com".
negative_variants.expected:
The confirmation message is identical to the message for a
known email — no "user not found" hint, no error.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Response is indistinguishable from the known-email response.

negative_variants.title: Reset link from one user cannot be used for another
negative_variants.action:
Request a reset for Sam Rivera. Take the token, attempt to use
it to reset Alex Morgan's password.
negative_variants.expected:
The token is rejected for any account other than the one it was
issued for.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Cross-account use of a reset token is refused.

title: Repeated failed sign-in attempts lock the account
goal:
Verify that a documented number of consecutive failed sign-in
attempts on a single account triggers a lockout — and that the
lockout is communicated, audit-logged, and recoverable.
preconditions: At least one user account exists.
The lockout policy (threshold, duration) is documented in the application's settings.

steps.action:
Read the lockout policy in settings. Note the threshold (e.g.,
5 failed attempts).
steps.expected:
Policy is visible and configurable. Defaults are sane (not
"999 attempts before lockout").

steps.action:
Try to sign in as Sam Rivera with the wrong password the
threshold number of times.
steps.expected:
Each attempt is rejected. After the Nth failure, the account
is locked.

steps.action: Try to sign in as Sam Rivera with the CORRECT password.
steps.expected:
Sign-in is still rejected with a clear "account locked" message
(NOT "wrong password" — that would let an attacker enumerate
lockouts).

steps.action: As the IT Admin, open the audit log.
steps.expected: Lockout event is recorded with user, timestamp, and source IP.

steps.action:
As the IT Admin, unlock the account from the user-management
area (or wait for the lockout duration to elapse and re-test).
steps.expected:
The unlock action is recorded. After unlock, a correct password
sign-in succeeds.

expected_overall: Lockout triggers, holds, audits, and is recoverable.
pass_criteria:
Threshold is enforced AND locked-account sign-in is rejected with
the right message AND unlock works AND both lock and unlock are
audited.

negative_variants.title: Lockout policy threshold cannot be set to 0 or absurdly high
negative_variants.action:
Try to set the lockout threshold to 0 attempts, then to 9999
attempts.
negative_variants.expected:
Both extremes are blocked or warn. The application enforces a
sensible minimum and maximum.
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Threshold is constrained to a defensible range with clear
messaging at the bounds.

negative_variants.title: Lockout does not affect other accounts
negative_variants.action:
After Sam Rivera is locked out, try to sign in as Alex Morgan
with the correct password.
negative_variants.expected:
Alex Morgan can sign in normally. Lockout is per-account, not
tenant-wide.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Other accounts remain accessible while one is locked.

negative_variants.title: Failed-sign-in attempts from many IPs still trigger lockout
negative_variants.action:
Have failed attempts come from two different source IPs (or
simulate via different sessions). Verify the count rolls up to
the account, not per-IP.
negative_variants.expected:
The threshold counts attempts against the user account
regardless of source IP, preventing distributed brute-force.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Account locks after the threshold is reached across sources.

title: Issue an API token for an integration user and revoke it
goal:
Verify a service / integration account can be issued an API token,
the token authenticates against the API, and revoking the token
immediately blocks further calls.
preconditions: The application has at least one documented API endpoint for integration use.
A non-human / service user account exists or can be created.

steps.action:
Create an integration account (e.g., "integration-quickbooks").
In its settings, generate an API token.
steps.expected:
Token is generated and shown ONCE to the admin. The application
makes clear the token cannot be retrieved later — it must be
stored externally now. The token has a defined scope (e.g.,
"read invoices") visible in settings.

steps.action:
Use the token to call any documented read-only endpoint (or
the application's "ping / whoami" endpoint).
steps.expected:
Call succeeds. The response shows the call was authenticated as
the integration account.

steps.action: Revoke the token from the user's settings.
steps.expected: Revoke succeeds. The token is removed from the list.

steps.action: Repeat the API call with the revoked token.
steps.expected: Call is rejected with a clear authentication error.

steps.action: Open the audit log.
steps.expected: Token issuance and revocation are both logged.

expected_overall: API tokens authenticate, are scoped, and are revocable in real time.
pass_criteria:
Token works while active AND fails immediately after revoke AND
both events audited.
notes:
If the application doesn't expose an API at all, this case can be
marked Blocked with a note. But "no API" is itself a finding worth
flagging — most ERP buyers expect at least a read-only integration
surface.

negative_variants.title: Token cannot exceed its declared scope
negative_variants.action: Use the read-only-scoped token to call a write endpoint.
negative_variants.expected:
The call is rejected with a clear scope-violation error. No
record is created or modified.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Out-of-scope call fails AND no side effect occurred.

negative_variants.title: Token is shown only once
negative_variants.action:
After generating the token, navigate away. Return to the user's
token list and try to view the generated token value.
negative_variants.expected:
The full token is no longer retrievable; only metadata (label,
scope, last-used) is shown.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Token value is not retrievable after the initial display.

negative_variants.title: Tampered token is rejected
negative_variants.action: Modify a single character in a valid token and submit it.
negative_variants.expected:
The call is rejected as unauthenticated. No partial
authentication is granted.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Tampered token is refused.

title: Configure single sign-on (SSO) for the tenant
goal:
Set up SAML / OIDC single sign-on against an external identity
provider and verify a federated user can sign in without a
separate local password.
preconditions: An external IdP test tenant is available (Azure AD, Okta, Google Workspace, etc.) with a test user.
A user record exists in the application matching the IdP user's email.

steps.action:
In tenant settings, find the SSO area. Choose the protocol
(SAML or OIDC).
steps.expected: Configuration form for the chosen protocol opens.

steps.action:
Configure the SSO with the IdP's metadata (entity ID, ACS URL,
certificate, claims mapping). Save.
steps.expected:
Configuration saves. The application provides a test-connection
affordance.

steps.action: Test the connection. Then sign out completely.
steps.expected: Test reports success.

steps.action:
At the sign-in page, choose the SSO option. Authenticate at the
IdP.
steps.expected:
After IdP authentication, the user is signed into the
application as the matching user with the right role.

steps.action:
Verify the audit log records the SSO sign-in with provider
detail.
steps.expected: Entry present with provider, user, and timestamp.

expected_overall: SSO works end-to-end. Federated user signs in successfully.
pass_criteria:
SSO completes AND user lands in the right role AND the sign-in is
audited.
notes:
If no IdP is available, this case can be marked Blocked with a
note. SSO is a common buyer requirement at mid-market scale.

negative_variants.title: SAML response with invalid signature is rejected
negative_variants.action:
Submit a SAML assertion whose signature does not match the
configured certificate.
negative_variants.expected:
The application rejects the assertion and surfaces a clear
"signature invalid" error in the audit log. No session created.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Invalid-signature assertion is refused AND audited.

negative_variants.title: Federated user with no local match cannot sign in
negative_variants.action:
Authenticate at the IdP as a user whose email has no matching
record in the application.
negative_variants.expected:
Sign-in is rejected with a clear message that the user has no
account in this tenant. No auto-provisioning unless explicitly
configured.
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Unmatched federated user is denied AND the rejection is
explained.

negative_variants.title: SSO config save with malformed metadata
negative_variants.action:
Save the SSO configuration with a missing or malformed entity
ID, ACS URL, or certificate.
negative_variants.expected:
Save is blocked with field-by-field error messages naming each
problem in plain English.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Save blocked AND the broken fields are individually identified.

title: Confirm that configured data survives a real restart
goal:
Make sure the configuration work done so far is genuinely saved,
not just held in memory or a session. This is a sanity check
before the company starts entering valuable data.
preconditions: All previous Phase 0 cases are completed.

steps.action:
Make a list (on paper or in another document) of:
  - The company name
  - The fiscal year start
  - The costing model
  - The five role names you created
  - The one user you invited
steps.expected:
You have a written reference for what should be present after
the test.

steps.action:
If possible, restart the application's server or service. If
the application is hosted and you can't restart it, instead
sign out, clear your browser data for this site (cookies and
local storage), and sign back in.
steps.expected:
You're back at a fresh sign-in screen, with no session
remembered.

steps.action:
Sign in as Alex Morgan. Go through the company settings and
compare what you see to your written list from step 1.
steps.expected:
Every value matches. Nothing was lost. No data appears scrambled
or partial.

steps.action:
Open the user list. Confirm Sam Rivera is still there with the
Floor Operator role.
steps.expected: Sam Rivera is in the user list with the correct role.

expected_overall:
All Phase 0 configuration survives a service restart and/or
browser data clear.
pass_criteria:
Every item on your written list was present and correct after the
restart.
why_this_matters:
Data loss after restart is a category of bug that destroys trust
in the application instantly. Catching it now, before any business
data has been entered, prevents a much worse loss later.

negative_variants.title: Sessions do not survive browser data clear
negative_variants.action:
After clearing site cookies and local storage, refresh the
application page without signing back in.
negative_variants.expected:
You see the sign-in screen. No prior session is automatically
restored, no privileged content renders, and no user data is
visible until you sign in again.
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Cleared browser produces sign-in prompt with no leaked session
state.

title: Open the application's starting page in a fresh browser
goal:
Confirm that someone with no account can reach a meaningful starting
page that explains what to do next.
preconditions: The application is installed and reachable at its expected URL.
No accounts have been created yet.
You are using a browser with no prior cookies or sign-in state for this application (a new private/incognito window works).

steps.action:
