Open a fresh private/incognito browser window. Navigate to the
application URL.
steps.expected:
A page loads. The page makes it clear what kind of application
this is and what someone arriving for the first time should do
next. There is a visible way to begin setup or create the first
account — not just a sign-in form for users who don't exist yet.

steps.action:
Read the page. Look for any text directed at a brand-new user
(someone who has never used this application before).
steps.expected:
You can identify, within 30 seconds of looking, what the next
step is. Either a clear "get started" or "set up your company"
action, or instructions explaining how the first user is created.

expected_overall:
A first-time visitor with no account has a clear path forward and
is not stuck staring at a sign-in form for an account that doesn't
exist.
pass_criteria: Within 30 seconds of the page loading, you knew what to do next.
why_this_matters:
An ERP that drops a brand-new buyer onto a sign-in page with no
setup path is a common failure. It usually means the install
documentation has to fill the gap, which means the application
isn't really finished.

negative_variants.title: Application URL with garbage path
negative_variants.action:
Navigate to the application URL with a nonsense path appended,
like /this-page-does-not-exist.
negative_variants.expected:
A clear error page appears (a "not found" page, in plain
language). It offers a way back to the main starting page. It
does NOT show a stack trace, raw error code, or developer-only
information.
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Error page is human-readable AND offers a way back AND does not
leak internal details.

title: Choose whether to connect to QuickBooks or use manual accounting
goal:
Decide how the system will record financial transactions: by
pushing them automatically to a connected accounting system like
QuickBooks, or by tracking them inside this application alone.
preconditions: Costing model is saved.

branches.prompt: How will this company handle accounting?

options.label: Skip for now (use a mock for testing)

steps.action:
In the settings area, find the section for accounting or
financial integrations.
steps.expected:
You see options for connecting to an external accounting
system (such as QuickBooks) AND an option for handling
accounting inside this application without an external
connection. Both options are clearly labeled. The trade-offs
between them are explained in plain English (not "ETL pipeline"
or "OAuth handshake required").

steps.action:
Select the QuickBooks option. Follow the steps the application
gives you to connect.
steps.expected:
The application explains what's about to happen before it
happens — for example, "you'll be sent to QuickBooks to sign
in." After connecting, a clear confirmation shows that the
connection is active and which QuickBooks company is connected.

steps.action: Select the manual option. Save.
steps.expected:
The setting saves. A confirmation explains what this means in
practice — for example, "financial transactions will be tracked
inside this application; you will export reports to share with
your accountant."

steps.action:
Select the option to use a mock or test mode for accounting.
Save.
steps.expected:
The application accepts this and clearly indicates it is in a
test mode for accounting (so the tester knows real financial
data isn't being pushed anywhere).

expected_overall:
Whichever option was chosen, the application clearly communicates
what that choice means and reflects the chosen mode in the
settings.
pass_criteria:
After saving, the chosen accounting mode is visible in settings AND
the application explained, in plain English, what was being chosen.
why_this_matters:
This is the case where Gemini's previous draft used jargon like
"API handshake" and "ETL." That language is exactly what loses a
non-technical controller during onboarding. The application is on
the hook to explain this in normal words.
notes:
If the chosen option requires entering credentials or account
numbers, do NOT enter real ones. The runner allows you to mark this
case as "would have done" and move on if you're testing the flow
without real connections.

negative_variants.title: Show meaningful error if QuickBooks connection fails
negative_variants.action:
If you chose QuickBooks: deliberately interrupt the connection
(for example, close the popup window mid-sign-in, or enter
wrong credentials).
negative_variants.expected:
The application explains in plain English what went wrong and
what to do next. It does NOT show a stack trace, raw error
code, or message like "OAuth callback failed: error 500."
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Failure was communicated in language a non-technical user would
understand AND offered a way to try again.

title: Choose which shipping carriers to connect
goal:
Decide which package carriers (UPS, FedEx, USPS, freight providers)
the system will connect to for live rates and label printing — or
configure for manual tracking entry.
preconditions: Costing model is saved.

steps.action: In settings, find the shipping carrier integrations area.
steps.expected:
A list of supported carriers is visible. Each carrier shows
whether it is currently connected. There is also an option for
"manual entry" — meaning the company will type tracking numbers
in by hand rather than getting them from a carrier connection.

steps.action:
Choose at least one carrier OR choose manual entry. If
connecting a carrier requires credentials you don't have, choose
manual entry for testing purposes.
steps.expected:
The choice saves. The settings page reflects which carriers (or
manual mode) is active.

expected_overall:
At least one shipping option is configured. The system knows how it
will handle outbound shipments later in the workflow.
pass_criteria: Shipping configuration is saved and visible in settings.
notes:
Real carrier connections are not required for testing. A tester
who chose "manual entry" here can later test that shipping cases
work even without a connected carrier — that is itself an
important capability.

negative_variants.title: Save shipping config with no carrier and no manual mode
negative_variants.action:
Try to save shipping configuration with neither a carrier
selected nor manual mode chosen.
negative_variants.expected:
The save is blocked with a clear message that at least one
shipping option must be selected, or manual mode is the
explicit fallback.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Save is blocked AND the requirement is named in plain English.

negative_variants.title: Carrier credential failure surfaces a usable error
negative_variants.action:
If you attempted a carrier connection: enter intentionally
wrong credentials.
negative_variants.expected:
The application reports the failure in plain English and
offers a way to retry. No stack trace, no raw HTTP status code
shown to the user.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Failure is reported clearly AND retry is available.

title: Choose how sales tax will be calculated
goal:
Set up the way the system figures sales tax on customer invoices.
This can be a connected service that calculates tax automatically,
or manually entered tax rates the company maintains itself.
preconditions: Company identity is saved.

steps.action: In settings, find the tax calculation area.
steps.expected:
Options are visible: at minimum, a way to manually enter tax
rates (by jurisdiction or as a flat rate), and ideally an
option to connect to an automatic tax service.

steps.action:
Choose manual tax rates. Add a single rate:
  Jurisdiction: Oregon
  Rate: 0%
  Effective date: today
Save.
steps.expected: The rate saves. The settings show one tax rate is now configured.
steps.notes:
Oregon doesn't have a state sales tax, so 0% is realistic for
this fictional company. Picking a real-world rate makes the
test data feel right.

expected_overall:
At least one tax rule is in place so future invoices have something
to apply.
pass_criteria: A tax configuration is saved and visible.

negative_variants.title: Reject negative or implausible tax rate
negative_variants.action: Try to save a rate of -5% or 250%.
negative_variants.expected:
The form blocks the save with a plain-language explanation that
tax rates must be between 0 and 100 (or another sensible cap).
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Out-of-range rate is rejected AND the bounds are explained.

negative_variants.title: Reject save with no jurisdiction
negative_variants.action: Try to save a tax rate with the jurisdiction field blank.
negative_variants.expected:
The save is blocked with a clear "jurisdiction is required"
message.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Save blocked AND the missing field is named.

negative_variants.title: Reject overlapping effective-date rates for same jurisdiction
negative_variants.action:
Add a second Oregon rate with the same effective date as the
first.
negative_variants.expected:
The save is blocked or warns that two rates cannot be effective
simultaneously for the same jurisdiction.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Overlap is detected and surfaced; user must resolve before save.

title: Enter the company name, address, and basic identity
goal:
Put the company's real name and address into the system so it can
appear on documents like invoices, purchase orders, and packing
slips later.
preconditions: You are signed in as the first admin.
No company-level settings have been entered.

steps.action: Open the company settings area.
steps.expected:
A form or page appears with fields for company name, address,
phone, and similar identity information.

steps.action:
Fill in:
  Company name: Cascade Components, LLC
  Address: 1820 Industrial Way, Eugene, OR 97402, USA
  Phone: (541) 555-0142
  Website: cascade-components.example
  Federal tax ID (or equivalent): 88-1234567
Save the form.
steps.expected:
A confirmation appears that the settings were saved. The values
you entered are still visible if you reload or revisit the page.

steps.action: Sign out completely. Sign back in.
steps.expected:
After signing back in, the company identity values you entered
are still saved. You did not have to re-enter them.

expected_overall: The company's identity is recorded and persists across sessions.
pass_criteria: Company name and address persist after signing out and back in.
why_this_matters:
Every document the system generates — invoices, POs, shipping
paperwork — uses this information. If it's missing or wrong,
every document is wrong.

negative_variants.title: Save with company name missing
negative_variants.action: Clear the company name field and try to save.
negative_variants.expected:
The form blocks the save. A clear message says the company name
is required.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Save was blocked AND the message was clear.

negative_variants.title: Reject malformed federal tax ID
negative_variants.action: Enter "abcd" in the federal tax ID field and try to save.
negative_variants.expected:
The form blocks the save with a plain-language error explaining
the expected format (e.g., 9 digits, optional dash).
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Save blocked AND error names the tax ID field with format
guidance.

negative_variants.title: Reject implausibly long company name
negative_variants.action:
Paste a 5,000-character string into the company name field and
try to save.
negative_variants.expected:
Either the field caps input at a sensible limit and explains the
cap, or the form rejects the save with a length error. The
application does NOT silently truncate without notice.
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Oversize input is rejected or capped with a clear message; no
silent truncation.

title: Set the company's primary language and regional formats
goal:
Configure the language the system displays in and the formats used
for dates, numbers, and currency.
preconditions: The company identity is saved (from P0-TENANT-001).

steps.action:
In the company settings, find the language and regional
settings. Note what's currently selected.
steps.expected:
You can see what language the application is currently set to
(probably English) and what regional formats it's using.

steps.action:
Change the language to Spanish (US) — labeled in the
application's own language list, however that's expressed there.
Save.
steps.expected:
The selection is accepted. After save, the application's text
appears in Spanish — menu labels, button text, page headings.

steps.action: Look around. Open at least three different pages or areas.
steps.expected:
Every page you visit is in Spanish. There are no pockets of
English text mixed in (other than data you typed, like the
company name). If you find any English text in menus, buttons,
or labels, note where.

steps.action:
Return to settings. Switch the language back to English (US).
Save.
steps.expected: The application is now back in English everywhere.

expected_overall:
The application can be switched between English and Spanish and
the change applies completely across all pages.
pass_criteria:
After switching to Spanish, no English text appeared in UI labels
on the three pages you checked. After switching back, no Spanish
remained.
why_this_matters:
A bilingual workforce relies on this working completely. Stray
untranslated strings — even just one button — cause real
confusion in production environments.
notes:
This case is intentionally a representative i18n check. The
dedicated i18n suite goes deeper. For this case, three pages worth
of spot-checking is enough.

negative_variants.title: Language switch must not corrupt entered data
negative_variants.action:
Switch to Spanish, then back to English. Open the company
identity record from P0-TENANT-001.
negative_variants.expected:
Entered values (company name, address, phone) are unchanged.
No characters are mangled or re-encoded.
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
All previously entered text matches what was saved, byte for
byte.

negative_variants.title: Reject unsupported locale value
negative_variants.action:
Try to set the locale to one the application does not list as
supported (e.g., bypass the dropdown via a direct API or URL).
negative_variants.expected:
The application either filters the option out entirely or
rejects the save with a clear "locale not supported" message.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Unsupported locale is not saved AND the rejection is explicit.

title: Set the primary time zone
goal:
Make sure timestamps displayed in the application use the company's
local time, not the server's time.
preconditions: Company identity is saved.

steps.action:
In the company settings, find the time zone selector. Set it to
"America/Los_Angeles" or "Pacific Time (US & Canada)" (whichever
label the application uses for the same zone). Save.
steps.expected: The setting is accepted and saved.

steps.action:
Find any place in the application that displays a timestamp —
for example, the "last saved" or "last signed in" time on your
own admin profile.
steps.expected:
The displayed time matches what the wall clock would show in
Pacific time, not UTC, not server time.

steps.action:
Change the time zone to "America/New_York" (Eastern). Save.
Reload the page where you saw the timestamp in step 2.
steps.expected:
The same timestamp now displays three hours later than it did
in step 2.

steps.action: Set the time zone back to Pacific. Save.
steps.expected: The timestamp returns to Pacific display.

expected_overall:
Displayed times respect the configured time zone. The same
underlying moment displays differently when the time zone changes.
pass_criteria:
The displayed timestamp shifted by exactly the correct number of
hours when the time zone was changed.
why_this_matters:
Time zone bugs are notorious. A scheduled production run that says
"2:00 PM" needs to mean 2:00 PM in the user's local time, not
some arbitrary server time. Getting this wrong silently is one of
the most expensive bug classes in any business application.

negative_variants.title: Reject invalid time zone value
negative_variants.action:
Attempt to save the time zone field with a free-text value that
is not a recognized IANA zone (e.g., "Pacific Time-ish").
negative_variants.expected:
The save is blocked with a clear "select a valid time zone"
message, or the field only accepts values from a controlled list.
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Invalid zone is not persisted AND the rejection is in plain
language.

negative_variants.title: Time zone change preserves stored UTC moments
negative_variants.action:
Note the displayed timestamp on the admin profile. Switch the
time zone from Pacific to Eastern. Reload.
negative_variants.expected:
The displayed clock face shifts by the correct offset, but the
underlying moment does not move. Switching back returns to the
original display.
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Display shifts by the correct offset AND the round trip back
restores the original display exactly.

title: Configure fiscal year start and primary currency
goal:
Set the company's accounting calendar and the currency it operates
in. This information shapes financial reports for the rest of the
company's life in the system.
preconditions: Company identity is saved.
No financial transactions have been recorded.

steps.action:
In the company settings, find the area for fiscal year and
currency settings.
steps.expected:
You can see fields for fiscal year start (a month, or month and
day) and primary currency.

steps.action:
Set fiscal year start to January 1. Set primary currency to
US Dollars (USD). Save.
steps.expected: Settings save. A confirmation appears.

steps.action:
Find any area of the application that displays monetary values
— even if it's a placeholder showing $0.00 somewhere.
steps.expected:
Monetary values display with the dollar sign and US-style
formatting (e.g. $1,234.56, not 1.234,56 € or 1234.56 USD).

expected_overall:
Fiscal year and currency are saved. Monetary displays use the
configured currency's symbol and formatting.
pass_criteria:
Currency formatting throughout the application matches the saved
setting (USD, with $ symbol and US thousands separator).
why_this_matters:
Changing primary currency after transactions exist requires
complicated data migration. Setting it correctly at the start
matters more than it seems.
notes:
Per design, the costing model setting (also fiscal/financial) is
set later in this phase. It deserves its own case because the
consequences of changing it later are severe.

negative_variants.title: Reject invalid fiscal year start
negative_variants.action:
Try to set fiscal year start to "Bluemonth 32" or an out-of-
range date.
negative_variants.expected:
The form blocks the save with a plain-English error naming the
problem. The field constrains input to valid month/day pairs.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Invalid fiscal start is not saved AND the error is clear.

negative_variants.title: Currency change blocked once transactions exist
negative_variants.action:
After this case completes, in a later phase once any monetary
record exists, return here and try to change the primary
currency.
negative_variants.expected:
The application either blocks the change with a clear "cannot
change currency after transactions exist" message, or requires
an explicit migration confirmation that names the consequences.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Silent currency change is impossible once transactions exist.

title: Choose how the company values its inventory and production
goal:
Pick the method the system uses to put a dollar value on inventory
and finished goods. This is a one-time choice that affects every
financial number the company produces.
preconditions: Fiscal year and currency are saved.
No inventory or production transactions exist.

steps.action:
In the company settings, find the area for costing or inventory
valuation settings.
steps.expected:
You can see the available costing models (common names include
"Standard Cost," "Average Cost," "FIFO," and "Actual Cost").
Each option has at least a short explanation in plain English
of what it means and when a company would pick it.
steps.notes:
If the options are listed but no explanation is provided, that
is a bug. A controller picking this setting needs to understand
what they're picking. "Just pick one" is not acceptable for a
decision this consequential.

steps.action: Read the explanations. Pick "Standard Cost." Save.
steps.expected:
The setting saves. A confirmation appears that includes a clear
warning, in plain language, that this setting is hard to change
later. The warning is not buried in fine print.

steps.action:
Try to change the setting to a different option (for example,
"Average Cost"). Attempt to save.
steps.expected:
The application either:
(a) blocks the change with a clear explanation that the costing
    model can't be changed without a special data migration
    process, or
(b) accepts the change but only after a strong warning that
    asks for explicit confirmation.

What is NOT acceptable: silently changing the costing model
with no warning.

steps.action:
Cancel any change you may have just made. Confirm Standard Cost
is still the saved setting.
steps.expected: Standard Cost is the active setting.

expected_overall:
A costing model is chosen, saved, and the system protects this
setting from being casually changed.
pass_criteria:
Standard Cost is saved AND attempting to change it produced either
a block or a strong warning.
why_this_matters:
Every inventory transaction from this point forward gets valued
according to this model. Quietly changing it later means every
historical number has a different meaning before and after the
change. This setting deserves protection.

negative_variants.title: Reject save with no costing model selected
negative_variants.action:
Clear the costing model selection (no option chosen) and try
to save.
negative_variants.expected:
The form blocks the save with a clear message that a costing
model is required.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Save is blocked AND the missing selection is named.

negative_variants.title: Costing model change requires deliberate confirmation
negative_variants.action:
After saving Standard Cost, attempt to switch to FIFO and
confirm the change immediately without reading the warning.
negative_variants.expected:
The application interrupts with an explicit "this changes how
every existing item is valued" confirmation that requires a
typed phrase or second click. Single-click silent change is not
acceptable.
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Costing model change requires explicit, deliberate confirmation
beyond a single click.

title: Set up the roles that will exist in this company
goal:
Define the named roles (titles like "Production Manager,"
"Floor Operator," "Bookkeeper") that will be assigned to users
later. A small shop may have only two or three; a large
organization may have a dozen or more.
preconditions: Admin account is signed in.
No additional users have been created yet.

steps.action: In settings, find the area for roles or permissions.
steps.expected:
A roles area is available. There may already be some default
roles (like "Administrator"). You can see a way to create new
roles.

steps.action:
Create the following roles. For each, give it a name and a
short description. Don't worry about specific permissions yet
— that's the next case.
  - Production Manager
  - Floor Operator
  - Warehouse Associate
  - Bookkeeper
  - Sales Representative
steps.expected:
Each role saves successfully. The roles list now includes these
five roles plus the default Administrator.

steps.action: Try to delete one of the roles you just created (any one).
steps.expected:
The application allows deletion of an unused role, or warns
that the role is referenced elsewhere (it shouldn't be — no
users have been assigned yet). What is NOT acceptable: silent
failure to delete with no message.

steps.action: If you deleted the role, recreate it.
steps.expected: The role can be recreated with the same name without issue.

expected_overall:
Five custom roles plus the default administrator role exist in
the system.
pass_criteria:
All five roles are visible in the roles list AND the role
creation/deletion behavior was clear and reversible.
why_this_matters:
The role taxonomy a company sets up here determines who can do
what for the rest of the company's life in the system. Getting
it adjustable from the start means small shops can collapse roles
and large shops can expand them.
notes:
Different test fixtures may swap this list. A small-shop fixture
might have only "Owner" and "Worker." An enterprise fixture might
have fifteen named roles. The principle being tested is: can the
company define whatever roles it needs without friction?

negative_variants.title: Reject duplicate role name
negative_variants.action: Try to create a second role named "Floor Operator."
negative_variants.expected:
The save is blocked with a clear message that the role name is
already in use.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Duplicate role rejected AND existing role is named.

negative_variants.title: Reject empty role name
negative_variants.action: Try to save a role with no name.
negative_variants.expected: The form blocks the save with a clear "name is required" error.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Save blocked AND missing field is named.

negative_variants.title: Block deletion of in-use role
negative_variants.action:
After P0-USER-002 assigns Floor Operator some permissions and
P0-USER-003 assigns it to a user, return here and try to delete
the Floor Operator role.
negative_variants.expected:
Deletion is blocked or warns that the role is in use, naming
the dependent users. No silent cascade-delete.
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Deletion blocked or explicitly confirmed; dependent users
remain assigned to a valid role.

title: Configure what one role can and cannot do
goal:
Take one of the roles you defined and assign it a meaningful set
of permissions. Verify that the permission system uses plain
language a non-technical person can understand.
preconditions: Roles are defined (from P0-USER-001).

steps.action: Open the "Floor Operator" role for editing.
steps.expected:
A page or panel shows the permissions assigned to this role.
Permissions are organized in a way that's understandable —
grouped by area of work (e.g., "Production," "Inventory") and
labeled in plain English (e.g., "Start a work order," "Report
time on a job") rather than technical terms (e.g.,
"WORKORDER_TRANSITION_TO_ACTIVE").

steps.action:
Grant this role permissions appropriate for a floor operator:
things like starting and completing work orders, reporting
labor time, and consuming materials. Deny permissions that
shouldn't apply: things like creating customers, approving
purchase orders, or changing pricing. Save.
steps.expected:
The save succeeds. The permissions you set are reflected when
you reopen the role.

steps.action:
Look for a feature that summarizes "what can someone with this
role actually do?" in plain language.
steps.expected:
Such a summary exists. Reading it tells you what a Floor
Operator can accomplish without you having to interpret
individual permission flags.
steps.notes:
If no such summary exists, that's a usability gap worth noting.
Permission systems that only show flags-and-checkboxes are
famously unusable for non-technical admins.

expected_overall:
The Floor Operator role now has a defined set of permissions in
plain language.
pass_criteria:
Permissions are saved AND the permission labels are
understandable to a non-technical person.

negative_variants.title: Permission change requires save to take effect
negative_variants.action:
Toggle a permission on the Floor Operator role but navigate
away without saving.
negative_variants.expected:
The application warns about unsaved changes or discards them
explicitly. Returning to the role shows the original permissions.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: No silent persistence of unsaved permission edits.

negative_variants.title: Cannot revoke own admin permissions to lock self out
negative_variants.action:
As the only administrator, try to remove the "manage users" or
"manage roles" permission from your own role.
negative_variants.expected:
The application blocks the change or warns that this would
orphan tenant administration. At minimum, one user must retain
admin capability.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Self-lockout is prevented AND the warning explains why.

title: Invite a second user and assign them a role
goal:
Verify that the first admin can create a second user, assign them
a role, and that the new user can sign in successfully.
preconditions: Roles are defined and at least one (Floor Operator) has permissions configured.

steps.action:
In the user management area, start the process to add a new
user.
steps.expected: A form appears asking for the new user's information.

steps.action:
Fill in:
  Name: Sam Rivera
  Email: sam.rivera@cascade-components.example
  Role: Floor Operator
Submit.
steps.expected:
The user is created. The application clearly indicates how the
new user will get their access — either an invitation email is
sent, a one-time setup link is shown, or a temporary password
is displayed for the admin to share. Whichever method, it's
clear what happens next.

steps.action:
Sign out as Alex Morgan. Using whatever method the application
provided in step 2 (invitation link, temporary credentials,
etc.), sign in as Sam Rivera and complete any required first-
time setup (like setting a password).
steps.expected:
Sam Rivera can successfully sign in. The application recognizes
them as a Floor Operator.

steps.action: As Sam Rivera, look around the application.
steps.expected:
Sam sees only what a Floor Operator should see. They cannot
reach areas they shouldn't have access to (like company
settings or user management). When they try, the application
either hides those areas entirely or explains, in plain
English, that they don't have access.

steps.action: Sign out. Sign back in as Alex Morgan.
steps.expected:
You're back in as the admin. Sam Rivera's user record is
still visible in the user list with their assigned role.

expected_overall:
A second user exists, can sign in, and is correctly limited to
what a Floor Operator should be able to do.
pass_criteria:
Sam Rivera signed in successfully AND was correctly limited to
Floor Operator capabilities.
why_this_matters:
This is the first real test of the permission system. If the
newly created user can do things they shouldn't, or can't do
things they should, the role configuration is broken.

negative_variants.title: Reject duplicate email
negative_variants.action:
As Alex Morgan, try to create another user with the same
email address as Sam Rivera.
negative_variants.expected:
The application blocks this with a clear message that the
email is already in use.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Creation was blocked AND the message was clear.

negative_variants.title: Reject user creation without a role
negative_variants.action: Try to create a third user with no role assigned.
negative_variants.expected: The save is blocked with a clear "role is required" message.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: User creation blocked when role is unset.

negative_variants.title: Reject malformed email at user creation
negative_variants.action: Try to create a new user with email "not-an-email".
negative_variants.expected:
The save is blocked with a plain-English message that the
email address is invalid.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Malformed email is rejected with a clear, non-technical message.

negative_variants.title: Invitation link is single-use
negative_variants.action:
After Sam Rivera completes their first sign-in, attempt to use
the original invitation link a second time (in another browser).
negative_variants.expected:
The link is rejected as expired or already used. It does NOT
grant a second session or reset the password silently.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Reused invitation link is refused AND the rejection is clear.

title: Provision multiple users from a CSV upload
goal:
Verify an IT Admin can create a batch of users (e.g., 25) from a
CSV file with names, emails, and roles, and that errors on
individual rows are flagged without blocking the whole import.
preconditions: At least one role exists for the new users to be assigned to.

steps.action: Find the user import area. Download the CSV template.
steps.expected: Template is available. Required columns are documented.

steps.action:
Fill in 25 plausible users — 23 valid, 2 with intentional
errors (one with an invalid email, one with a non-existent
role). Upload the file.
steps.expected:
The application shows a preview / validation summary. The 2
bad rows are flagged with line number and the reason. Valid
rows are queued.

steps.action: Confirm the import.
steps.expected:
23 users are created. Invitations or temporary credentials are
issued. The 2 bad rows are not silently skipped — they appear
in an error list with actionable detail.

steps.action: Open the user list. Verify the 23 new users.
steps.expected: All 23 visible with their assigned roles.

expected_overall: Bulk user provisioning works with row-level error reporting.
pass_criteria:
23 users created AND 2 bad rows reported with reasons AND no
silent partial failures.

negative_variants.title: Reject CSV with missing required columns
negative_variants.action: Upload a CSV missing the "email" column.
negative_variants.expected:
The import is blocked at validation with a clear message naming
the missing required column. No users are created.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Import blocked AND missing column is named.

negative_variants.title: Reject CSV with duplicate emails inside the file
negative_variants.action: Upload a CSV where two rows share the same email address.
negative_variants.expected:
The duplicates are flagged at validation. The application
either rejects the import or imports the first occurrence and
reports the second as a row-level error.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Duplicate rows are flagged AND the resolution is explicit.

negative_variants.title: Reject oversized upload
negative_variants.action:
Upload a CSV well above the documented row limit (e.g.,
100,000 rows if the limit is 5,000).
negative_variants.expected:
The import is rejected at upload with a clear "file too large"
or "row count exceeds limit" message naming the limit.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Oversize upload refused AND the limit is communicated.

title: Create the first asset record
goal:
Add a piece of equipment as a fixed asset. Asset records will be
referenced by work orders that use the equipment, by PM schedules,
and by depreciation calculations later.
preconditions: At least one work center exists (P1-WC-001).
At least one GL account for fixed assets exists (P1-GL-001).

steps.action:
Find and open the assets area. (Common labels: "Assets,"
"Equipment," "Fixed Assets.")
steps.expected: The assets list appears, empty by default.

steps.action:
Choose to create a new asset. Enter:
- Name: "Press 1 - 60 ton hydraulic"
- Asset tag / serial: a plausible identifier
- Location: {{primary_location}} - Main Plant
- Work center: Press Shop
- Acquisition date: a plausible past date
- Acquisition cost: a plausible amount (e.g., $45,000)
- Asset GL account: an asset account from P1-GL-001
- Depreciation method: straight-line, 10-year life
steps.expected: The form accepts the values and saves.

steps.action: Open the asset record you just created.
steps.expected:
All entered values are visible. The asset is linked to the
Press Shop work center. A depreciation schedule is generated
or available to view.

expected_overall:
The asset exists, is linked to a work center, and has the
financial details needed for depreciation. Work orders can now
reference it.
pass_criteria:
Asset record exists AND links to the correct work center AND
shows a depreciation schedule (or offers to generate one).

negative_variants.title: Reject negative or zero acquisition cost
negative_variants.action: Try to save an asset with acquisition cost of $0 or -$5,000.
negative_variants.expected:
Save is blocked with a plain-language error explaining cost
must be positive.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Non-positive acquisition cost is refused.

negative_variants.title: Reject zero or negative useful life
negative_variants.action: Try to save with useful life of 0 years or -10 years.
negative_variants.expected: Save is blocked. Useful life must be a positive integer.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Invalid useful life is refused.

negative_variants.title: Reject duplicate asset tag
negative_variants.action:
Try to create a second asset with the same asset tag / serial
as an existing asset.
negative_variants.expected: Save is blocked with a clear "asset tag already in use" message.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Duplicate asset tag is refused.

title: Create additional assets across multiple work centers
goal:
Build out a small asset list and verify filtering by location and
work center works as expected.
preconditions: At least one fixed asset record exists, linked to a work center, with depreciation method and useful life configured and a depreciation schedule generated. (Established by P1-ASSET-001.)

steps.action:
Create the following assets:
- "MIG Welder #2" — work center: Weld
- "Paint Booth A" — work center: Paint
- "CNC Mill - Haas VF-2" — work center: Cut & Saw
steps.expected:
Each asset saves successfully and links to the named work
center.

steps.action: Open the assets list. Filter or sort by work center.
steps.expected:
All four assets appear (Press 1 plus the three new ones).
Filtering by work center shows only the assets in that work
center.

steps.action: Filter by location.
steps.expected: All four assets remain (all are at the primary location).

expected_overall:
Four assets exist, each linked to its work center. Filters work
predictably.
pass_criteria:
Four assets total AND filter by work center returns the right
subset for each.

negative_variants.title: Cannot link asset to a deleted work center
negative_variants.action:
Delete (or deactivate) a work center that an asset references,
then reload the asset.
negative_variants.expected:
The application either prevents deletion of an in-use work
center OR clearly flags the asset as needing reassignment. No
silent dangling reference.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: No asset references a non-existent work center.

title: Verify inventory and fixed assets are differentiated at PO time
goal:
Confirm that when a purchase order is created, line items can be
classified as either inventory (will be consumed or sold) or fixed
asset (will be capitalized and depreciated). Get this right at PO
entry time, not after the fact.
preconditions: The chart of accounts is initialized with standard accounts: cash, accounts receivable, inventory, accounts payable, sales, cost of goods sold, and wages — at minimum. (Established by P1-GL-001.)
At least one vendor exists (covered in Phase 2 — for now, this case can be deferred or run with a placeholder vendor).

steps.action: Find the purchase order area. Choose to create a new PO.
steps.expected: The PO creation form opens with line items available.

steps.action:
Add two line items:
- Line 1: 100 units of a part (inventory)
- Line 2: 1 piece of equipment (fixed asset, e.g., "60-ton
  press, model XYZ")
steps.expected:
For each line, the form prompts for or distinguishes between
inventory and fixed-asset classification. The fixed-asset line
requires (or accepts) acquisition cost, asset class, and a
capitalization GL account.

steps.action:
Save the PO as a draft. Re-open it. Verify line classifications
persist.
steps.expected:
Line 1 shows as inventory; Line 2 shows as a fixed asset with
the capitalization details preserved.

expected_overall:
POs distinguish between inventory and fixed-asset purchases at the
line level. Receipt against each line will eventually post to the
correct account (inventory vs. capital asset).
pass_criteria:
PO can carry both line types AND the distinction persists across
save and reload.
why_this_matters:
ERPs that don't enforce this at PO time end up with fixed assets
posted to inventory accounts (or vice versa) and require manual
journal-entry corrections. Catching the bug at PO entry is far
cheaper than catching it at month-end close.
notes:
This case may not be fully runnable until Phase 2 (master data,
including vendors). Run as far as you can and stop at the first
step that needs a vendor or part record that doesn't exist yet.
Re-run after Phase 2 if needed.

negative_variants.title: Fixed-asset line requires capitalization account
negative_variants.action:
Add a fixed-asset line item to a PO with the capitalization
account left blank. Try to save.
negative_variants.expected:
Save is blocked with a clear "fixed-asset line requires a
capitalization account" message.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Save is refused until a valid asset account is selected.

negative_variants.title: Cannot reclassify line type after PO is approved
negative_variants.action:
Approve the PO. Then try to switch a line from inventory to
fixed-asset.
negative_variants.expected:
The change is blocked or requires a documented amendment
process; silent reclassification post-approval is not
acceptable.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Reclassification post-approval is gated.

title: Configure the plant default calendar
goal:
Set up the default working schedule the plant runs on (which days,
which shifts, what's a holiday). Other capacity calculations
reference this calendar.
preconditions: At least one location exists (P1-LOC-001).

steps.action:
Find and open the calendars area. (Common labels: "Calendars,"
"Schedules," "Shop Calendar.")
steps.expected:
A calendar configuration screen appears. There may be a
pre-existing default calendar to edit, or a prompt to create one.

steps.action:
Configure or edit the default calendar:
- Working days: Monday through Friday
- Shifts: one shift, 8 hours, e.g. 7:00 AM to 3:30 PM with a
  30-minute lunch
- Mark a few US federal holidays for the upcoming year as
  non-working days (e.g., Independence Day, Thanksgiving,
  Christmas).
steps.expected:
The calendar accepts the configuration and saves. The visible
schedule reflects the working days, shift times, and holidays.

steps.action:
Open a work center detail page (from P1-WC-001) and verify it
uses this calendar by default.
steps.expected: The work center shows the default calendar is active.

expected_overall:
The plant default calendar is configured. Capacity calculations
for any work center using the default will reflect the configured
shift hours and holidays.
pass_criteria:
Calendar saved successfully AND work centers default to it AND
configured holidays are observed in the visible schedule.

negative_variants.title: Reject shift end-time before start-time
negative_variants.action:
Try to configure a shift with start 3:00 PM and end 7:00 AM the
same day (i.e., end-before-start, not crossing midnight).
negative_variants.expected:
Submission is blocked with a clear "end must be after start"
message. Cross-midnight shifts (e.g., 11:00 PM to 7:00 AM) are
configured via an explicit "crosses midnight" flag, not implied.
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
End-before-start blocked AND cross-midnight has explicit
handling.

negative_variants.title: Reject overlapping shifts on the same day
negative_variants.action:
Try to add a second shift overlapping the existing first shift
(e.g., add 12:00 PM-8:00 PM when the existing shift is 7:00 AM-
3:30 PM).
negative_variants.expected:
Application either blocks the overlap or warns clearly,
requiring explicit confirmation.
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Overlap is blocked or flagged for explicit confirmation, not
silently accepted.

title: Configure a second-shift calendar
goal:
Set up an alternative calendar for areas that run a second shift,
used by P1-WC-003 and similar overrides.
preconditions: The plant default calendar is configured with working days, shift times, and at least the upcoming-year US federal holidays marked non-working. (Established by P1-CAL-001.)

steps.action: Open the calendars area. Choose to create a new calendar.
steps.expected: The calendar creation form appears.

steps.action:
Configure:
- Name: "Second Shift"
- Working days: Monday through Friday
- Shifts: one shift from 3:30 PM to 11:00 PM with a 30-minute
  meal break
- Inherit holidays from the plant default calendar (or
  re-enter the same holidays).
steps.expected:
The calendar saves. It is selectable as an override on work
center records.

expected_overall:
A second-shift calendar exists, distinguishable from the default,
and assignable to work centers that need to run on a different
schedule.
pass_criteria:
Second-shift calendar exists AND its shift times differ from the
plant default AND it can be selected on a work center record.

negative_variants.title: Reject duplicate calendar name
negative_variants.action: Try to save a second calendar named "Second Shift."
negative_variants.expected: Save is blocked with a clear "name must be unique" message.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Duplicate calendar name is refused.

title: Add a planned downtime block to a calendar
goal:
Reserve time on the calendar that's not a holiday but isn't
available for production either (planned maintenance, facility
shutdown, training day).
preconditions: The plant default calendar is configured with working days, shift times, and at least the upcoming-year US federal holidays marked non-working. (Established by P1-CAL-001.)

steps.action: Open the plant default calendar.
steps.expected: The calendar opens for editing.

steps.action:
Add a planned downtime block:
- Start: a Friday afternoon two weeks from now
- End: end of day
- Reason: "Quarterly facility maintenance"
steps.expected: The downtime block saves and is visible on the calendar.

steps.action:
Verify capacity for that day reflects the lost time. Open a
capacity report or work center capacity view for that day.
steps.expected:
Available capacity for the affected work centers is reduced
to reflect the lost hours.

expected_overall:
Planned downtime is recorded and capacity calculations subtract
it appropriately.
pass_criteria:
Downtime block visible on the calendar AND capacity for the
affected day is reduced.
why_this_matters:
Schedulers need to know not just "we don't run on Christmas" but
also "the paint booth is down for maintenance Friday afternoon."
Treating planned downtime as a first-class concept on calendars
prevents over-promising delivery dates.

negative_variants.title: Reject downtime block ending before it starts
negative_variants.action:
Try to add a downtime block whose end is earlier than its
start.
negative_variants.expected: Save is blocked with a clear "end must be after start" error.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Inverted-time downtime is refused.

negative_variants.title: Reject downtime block in the past with scheduled work
negative_variants.action:
Add a downtime block in the past on a day where production was
already scheduled. (May be deferred until a later phase has
scheduled work.)
negative_variants.expected:
The application warns that retroactive downtime conflicts with
already-scheduled work and requires explicit confirmation.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Retroactive downtime surfaces conflicts before applying.

title: Create the first employee record
goal:
Add the founder/first administrator as an employee. Employee records
are separate from system users — every employee may not have a
login, and every user may not be an employee.
preconditions: The first administrator has a system account (P0-ADMIN-001).
At least one location exists (P1-LOC-001).

steps.action:
Find and open the employees area. (Common labels: "Employees,"
"People," "Team.")
steps.expected: The employees list appears, empty by default.

steps.action:
Choose the action to create a new employee. Enter:
- First and last name
- Hire date (today or a plausible date in the past)
- Default location: {{primary_location}} - Main Plant
- Department or role title (e.g., "Owner / Admin")
- Pay type: salaried or hourly with a plausible rate
steps.expected: The form accepts the values and saves.

steps.action: Open the employee record you just created.
steps.expected:
All entered values are visible. The record carries an ID. There
is a way to link this employee to a system user account
(separately or in a follow-up step).

expected_overall:
The first employee exists, with a location, pay info, and a stable
ID. Can be referenced by labor records and time tracking later.
pass_criteria:
Employee record exists AND links to the primary location AND has
pay information set.
why_this_matters:
An "employee" and a "user" are different things. The cleaning crew
has employee records but no system access. A consultant has a
system user but no employee record. ERPs that conflate the two
break in subtle ways when this distinction matters.

negative_variants.title: Reject hire date far in the future
negative_variants.action: Try to set hire date to one year from today and save.
negative_variants.expected:
Submission is blocked or warns. The application does not
silently accept a hire date that would distort labor reports.
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Future-dated hire is blocked or surfaces a warning that
requires explicit override.

negative_variants.title: Reject negative pay rate
negative_variants.action: Try to set pay rate to a negative value.
negative_variants.expected: Submission is blocked with a clear error.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Negative pay rate rejected.

title: Link an employee record to a system user
goal:
Connect the first employee record to the first administrator's
system user account, so labor and audit trails attribute to the
same person consistently.
preconditions: The first employee record exists with hire date, default location, department, and pay info. (Established by P1-EMP-001.)

steps.action: Open the employee record from P1-EMP-001.
steps.expected: The employee detail view opens.

steps.action:
Find the linkage to a system user (may be on the main detail
page or a separate "Account" tab). Link to the first
administrator's user.
steps.expected:
The link is created. The user's email or username is now
visible on the employee record.

steps.action:
Open the system user record and verify the reverse link is
present.
steps.expected: The user record shows it is linked to the employee record.

expected_overall:
Employee and user records cross-reference each other. Reports that
combine the two (e.g., "labor hours by employee, who else has system
access") work consistently.
pass_criteria:
Both directions of the link are visible AND removing one removes
the other (or at least surfaces a warning).

negative_variants.title: Cannot link the same user to multiple employees
negative_variants.action:
Create a second employee record. Try to link the first
administrator's user account to it as well.
negative_variants.expected:
The link is blocked with a clear message that this user is
already linked to another employee.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Many-to-one is refused; user-employee link is one-to-one.

negative_variants.title: Cannot link an employee to a deactivated user
negative_variants.action:
Deactivate a user (per P1-USER-003), then try to link a new
employee record to it.
negative_variants.expected:
The link is blocked or warns that the target user is
deactivated.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Linking to deactivated users is refused or surfaces a warning.

title: Add additional employees with varied pay types
goal:
Build out a small employee roster covering the variety of pay
structures the system needs to support: hourly production, salaried
management, exempt vs. non-exempt.
preconditions: The first employee record exists with hire date, default location, department, and pay info. (Established by P1-EMP-001.)

steps.action:
Create three more employees:
- One hourly production worker with an overtime-eligible pay
  rate.
- One salaried-exempt manager (no overtime).
- One salaried-non-exempt supervisor (eligible for overtime
  even though salaried).
steps.expected:
Each employee saves successfully. Pay-type / exemption
classifications are captured.

steps.action: Open the employees list.
steps.expected:
All four employees appear (the original from P1-EMP-001 plus
the three new ones). Pay type and exemption are visible or
filterable.

expected_overall:
A small but representative employee roster exists. Records cover
the three main pay-classification combinations.
pass_criteria:
Four employees total AND each one's pay-type and exemption fields
reflect what was entered.
why_this_matters:
Many ERPs handle hourly and salaried but get exempt vs. non-exempt
classification wrong, which leads to overtime miscalculations.
Verifying both options exist and stick is worth the extra minute.

negative_variants.title: Reject duplicate employee identifier
negative_variants.action:
Try to create a second employee with the same employee number
/ identifier as an existing one.
negative_variants.expected: Save is blocked with a clear "ID already in use" message.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Duplicate employee identifier rejected.

negative_variants.title: Salary value cannot be zero on a salaried employee
negative_variants.action: Try to save a salaried employee with $0 salary.
negative_variants.expected:
Save is blocked or warns; salaried employees must have a
non-zero pay rate.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Zero-salary salaried employee refused or flagged.

title: Confirm the chart of accounts is initialized
goal:
Verify that a default chart of accounts exists. Without it, every
later financial transaction will fail.
preconditions: The application is bootstrapped: company identity, locale, time zone, fiscal year, currency, costing model, and integrations are configured, and the first administrator can sign in. (Phase 0 outcomes.)
The costing model and currency are configured (P0-TENANT-005).

steps.action:
Find and open the chart of accounts area. (Common labels: "Chart
of Accounts," "GL Accounts," "Accounts.")
steps.expected:
A list of accounts appears. Either pre-populated with a standard
chart, or the system offers to seed one based on the company's
industry or tax jurisdiction.

steps.action:
If the chart is empty, choose to seed it with a standard
chart-of-accounts template.
steps.expected:
The standard chart loads. It includes asset accounts (cash,
receivables, inventory), liability accounts (payables), revenue,
and expense accounts at minimum.

steps.action:
Spot-check the chart: search for "Cash," "Accounts Receivable,"
"Inventory," "Sales," "Cost of Goods Sold," "Wages."
steps.expected:
Each of these is present, with a unique account number and
classification (asset, liability, equity, revenue, expense).

expected_overall:
A complete chart of accounts exists, ready for transactions to
reference.
pass_criteria:
Chart contains entries for cash, receivables, inventory, payables,
sales, COGS, and wages — at minimum.
why_this_matters:
Some ERPs ship without a default chart and require you to create
every account by hand. That's a 1–2 day setup task that's easy to
get wrong. A seeded chart prevents the mistake.

negative_variants.title: Re-seeding chart does not duplicate existing accounts
negative_variants.action:
After seeding once, attempt to seed the chart again from the
same template.
negative_variants.expected:
The application warns that the chart is already populated and
either skips, merges with explicit confirmation, or refuses.
No silent duplicates appear in the account list.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: No duplicate accounts created on re-seed.

negative_variants.title: Cannot delete a system-required account
negative_variants.action:
Try to delete the system "Cash" or "Accounts Receivable"
account.
negative_variants.expected:
Deletion is blocked with a clear "this is a system-required
account" message.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: System-required accounts cannot be removed.

title: Add a custom GL account
goal:
Verify the system supports adding accounts beyond the seed chart, in
case the business has tracking needs the standard chart doesn't
cover.
preconditions: The chart of accounts is initialized with standard accounts: cash, accounts receivable, inventory, accounts payable, sales, cost of goods sold, and wages — at minimum. (Established by P1-GL-001.)

steps.action: Open the chart of accounts. Choose to add a new account.
steps.expected:
A form appears with fields for account number, name, type
(asset / liability / etc.), and possibly a parent account.

steps.action:
Add an account: number "5150," name "Subcontractor Costs," type
Expense.
steps.expected: The form accepts the values and saves.

steps.action: Search for "Subcontractor" in the chart.
steps.expected: The new account appears in the results.

expected_overall:
Custom account exists and behaves the same as seeded accounts —
searchable, postable to, and visible in reports.
pass_criteria: Account is created AND searchable AND its type is correctly recorded.

negative_variants.title: Reject duplicate account number
negative_variants.action: Try to create a second account with number "5150."
negative_variants.expected:
The form blocks submission and explains the number is already
in use.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Submission is blocked AND the duplicate is named.

title: Create the primary business location
goal:
Add the company's main physical site so other records (work centers,
inventory, employees) can reference it.
preconditions: The application is bootstrapped: company identity, locale, time zone, fiscal year, currency, costing model, and integrations are configured, and the first administrator can sign in. (Phase 0 outcomes.)
You are signed in as an administrator.
No locations have been created yet.

steps.action:
Find and open the locations area. (Common labels: "Locations,"
"Sites," "Facilities.")
steps.expected: The locations list appears, empty by default.

steps.action:
Choose the action to create a new location. Enter:
- Name: "{{primary_location}} - Main Plant"
- Address: a plausible street address in {{primary_location}}
- Phone: a plausible phone number
- Type: production / primary
steps.expected: The form accepts the values and saves successfully.

steps.action: Mark this location as the company's default or primary location.
steps.expected: A primary or default flag is visible on the location record.

expected_overall:
The primary location exists with a complete address and is marked as
the default. Other records can now reference it.
pass_criteria: Location is created AND visible in the list AND flagged as primary.

negative_variants.title: Reject location with missing required fields
negative_variants.action: Try to create a second location with only a name (no address).
negative_variants.expected:
The form blocks submission and explains which fields are required,
in plain language.
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Submission was blocked AND the error message names the missing
field(s) clearly.

title: Create a second location
goal:
Add a non-primary location to verify the system handles multi-site
operations.
preconditions: The primary location exists with a complete address and is flagged as the company default. (Established by P1-LOC-001.)

steps.action:
Find and open the locations area. Choose the action to create a
new location.
steps.expected: The location creation form appears.

steps.action:
Enter:
- Name: "Springfield Warehouse"
- Address: a plausible warehouse address in a different city
- Phone: a plausible phone number
- Type: warehouse / non-primary
steps.expected: The form accepts the values and saves.

steps.action: Open the locations list.
steps.expected:
Both locations appear. The primary location is visually
distinguished from the secondary.

expected_overall:
Two locations exist. The primary remains the default; the secondary
is available for reference but not flagged as primary.
pass_criteria: Both locations exist AND only the primary carries the default flag.
why_this_matters:
Many ERPs handle a single location well and break in subtle ways
when a second is added (records that silently default to "the only
location," reports that don't filter by location). Catching this
early saves a lot of rework.

negative_variants.title: Reject duplicate location name
negative_variants.action:
Try to create a third location with the same name as the
primary.
negative_variants.expected: Save is blocked with a clear "name must be unique" message.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Duplicate name rejected AND existing record is identified.

negative_variants.title: Cannot demote the only primary
negative_variants.action:
Try to remove the primary flag from the primary location while
no other location is flagged primary.
negative_variants.expected:
The change is blocked or warns that exactly one primary
location must exist.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Tenant is never left with zero primary locations.

title: Configure a sales tax code
goal:
Set up a tax classification for sales in the company's primary tax
jurisdiction.
preconditions: Tax calculation has been configured: a tax provider has been chosen (integrated provider or manual rates) and the choice persists across tenant settings. (Established by P0-INTEG-003.)
The chart of accounts is initialized.

steps.action:
Find and open the tax codes area. (Common labels: "Tax Codes,"
"Tax Rates," "Tax Configuration.")
steps.expected: A list of tax codes appears, possibly empty or with seed entries.

steps.action:
Create a new tax code:
- Name: "OR Sales Tax" (or wherever the primary location is)
- Rate: a plausible rate for the jurisdiction (e.g., 0% for
  Oregon, 7.25% for California base)
- Posting account: link to the appropriate liability account
  from the chart of accounts.
steps.expected: The form accepts the values and saves.

steps.action:
Verify the tax code is selectable on a sample customer or
sales-side record.
steps.expected: The tax code appears in the dropdown wherever taxes are applied.

expected_overall:
At least one tax code exists, has a defined rate, and posts to a
real GL account.
pass_criteria:
Tax code is created AND has a non-error posting account AND is
selectable on customer / order records.

negative_variants.title: Reject tax code without a posting account
negative_variants.action: Try to save a tax code with the GL posting account left blank.
negative_variants.expected:
Save is blocked with a clear "posting account is required"
message.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Save blocked AND missing field is named.

negative_variants.title: Reject tax code with rate above 100%
negative_variants.action: Try to save a tax code with a rate of 250%.
negative_variants.expected:
Save is blocked or warns; the rate must fall within a sensible
bound.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Out-of-range rate refused.

negative_variants.title: Posting account must be a liability
negative_variants.action: Try to set the posting account to a Revenue or Expense account.
negative_variants.expected:
The application either filters the dropdown to liability
accounts or rejects the save with a clear "tax must post to a
liability account" message.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Tax cannot post to a non-liability account.

title: Configure a tax-exempt classification
goal:
Set up a tax code that records "no tax was charged" on transactions
that are legitimately exempt (resale, government, out-of-state).
preconditions: At least one sales tax code exists with a defined rate, exemption flag, and posting GL account. (Established by P1-TAX-001.)

steps.action: Open the tax codes area. Create a new code.
steps.expected: The tax code form appears.

steps.action:
Enter:
- Name: "Tax Exempt - Resale"
- Rate: 0%
- Mark as exempt (if there's a flag separate from the rate)
steps.expected: The form accepts the values and saves.

expected_overall:
An exempt classification exists and is distinguishable on reports
from "0% rate that just happens to compute to zero."
pass_criteria:
Exempt code is created AND distinguishable from regular zero-rate
codes.
why_this_matters:
Tax authorities expect exempt sales to be reported as exempt, not as
"we charged 0%." Conflating the two is a common audit finding.

negative_variants.title: Exempt classification requires exemption reason
negative_variants.action:
Try to save a tax-exempt code without selecting or entering an
exemption reason / category.
negative_variants.expected:
Save is blocked or warns that an exemption reason is required
for audit purposes.
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Exempt code without reason is rejected or surfaces an explicit
warning.

title: Confirm standard units of measure are available
goal:
Verify that common units (each, kg, lb, hour, foot, square foot,
gallon, liter) exist out of the box. Most ERPs ship with these; if
yours doesn't, it's a setup gap to flag.
preconditions: The application is bootstrapped: company identity, locale, time zone, fiscal year, currency, costing model, and integrations are configured, and the first administrator can sign in. (Phase 0 outcomes.)

steps.action:
Find and open the units of measure area. (Common labels: "Units
of Measure," "UOM," "Measurement Units.")
steps.expected:
A list of units appears. The list is either pre-populated with
common units OR offers to seed them.

steps.action:
Verify the following are present (or seed them if the system
offers): each, kilogram, pound, hour, foot, square foot, gallon,
liter.
steps.expected: All units listed are available.

expected_overall:
The unit list contains the common units a fabrication shop will need
for parts, assemblies, and labor.
pass_criteria: All listed units are present after this case completes.
why_this_matters:
Inventing missing units one-by-one as parts are added is slow and
inconsistent. Confirming the standard set exists early avoids that.

negative_variants.title: Cannot delete a built-in unit that is in use
negative_variants.action:
Try to delete the "each" unit (a default that other records
will reference once parts exist).
negative_variants.expected:
Deletion is blocked or warns that the unit is built-in /
referenced. No silent removal that breaks downstream forms.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Built-in unit deletion is refused with a clear reason.

title: Add a custom unit of measure
goal:
Verify the system supports adding business-specific units beyond the
defaults.
preconditions: Standard units of measure are available (each, kilogram, pound, hour, foot, square foot, gallon, liter). (Established by P1-UOM-001.)

steps.action: Open the units of measure area. Choose to add a new unit.
steps.expected:
A form appears with fields for name, abbreviation, and possibly
a category (length, weight, volume, etc.).

steps.action:
Add a "sheet" unit (abbreviation: sht), categorized as count or
pieces.
steps.expected: The unit saves successfully and appears in the list.

expected_overall:
The custom unit exists alongside the defaults and is selectable when
creating parts later.
pass_criteria: Custom unit is created AND visible in the list.

negative_variants.title: Reject duplicate UoM abbreviation
negative_variants.action:
Try to add another custom unit with the same abbreviation as an
existing unit (e.g., "ea" if "each" already uses "ea").
negative_variants.expected:
Submission is blocked with a clear message that the abbreviation
is already in use.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Submission blocked AND duplicate is named.

title: Define a unit conversion
goal:
Set up a conversion factor between two units (e.g., 1 sheet = 32
square feet) so that the system can convert quantities between
measurements when issuing or receiving inventory.
preconditions: Standard units of measure exist (each, kilogram, pound, hour, foot, square foot, gallon, liter) plus at least one custom unit (e.g. "sheet"). (Established by P1-UOM-001 and P1-UOM-002.)

steps.action:
Find the unit conversions area. (May be a separate screen or a
tab on the unit detail page.)
steps.expected: A way to define conversions appears.

steps.action: Define: 1 sheet = 32 square feet.
steps.expected:
The conversion saves. Both directions of the conversion are
visible (sheet to square feet and square feet to sheet).

steps.action:
In a part record (or a calculator if the system provides one),
enter "2 sheets" and verify it equals 64 square feet.
steps.expected: The conversion is applied correctly.

expected_overall:
The conversion exists and is correctly applied wherever the system
needs to convert between sheet and square foot.
pass_criteria:
Round-trip conversion (e.g., 2 sht to 64 sqft to 2 sht) is exact,
no rounding loss.
why_this_matters:
Lots of real production processes consume material in a different
unit than they purchase it (buy coil by the foot, consume by the
pound). Wrong conversions are a top source of inventory variance.

negative_variants.title: Reject zero or negative conversion factor
negative_variants.action:
Try to define "1 sheet = 0 square feet" or "1 sheet = -32
square feet."
negative_variants.expected:
Save is blocked with a plain-English message that conversion
factors must be positive.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Non-positive factor is rejected.

negative_variants.title: Reject conversion across incompatible categories
negative_variants.action: Try to define "1 hour = 5 kilograms" (time to mass).
negative_variants.expected:
The application either blocks or warns clearly that a
conversion across categories is meaningless.
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Cross-category conversion is refused or surfaces an explicit
warning.

negative_variants.title: Reject conflicting reverse conversion
negative_variants.action:
After saving "1 sheet = 32 sqft", try to save "1 sheet = 50
sqft" (a contradicting factor).
negative_variants.expected:
The save is blocked or asks the user to confirm overwriting
the prior conversion.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Conflicting conversion is detected and surfaced.

title: Invite a second system user with a non-admin role
goal:
Create a second user account assigned to a less-privileged role,
to verify role-based access actually restricts what the user can
see and do.
preconditions: At least one non-admin role exists (P0-USER-001).
At least four employees exist covering varied pay classifications (hourly with overtime, salaried-exempt, salaried-non-exempt). (Established by P1-EMP-003.)

steps.action: Find and open the users area. Choose to invite a new user.
steps.expected: The invite form appears with fields for email, name, and role.

steps.action:
Invite a user with email "operator@example.com" assigned to a
Floor Operator role (or equivalent non-admin role from P0).
steps.expected:
The invite is sent (or the user is created directly with a
temporary credential, depending on your auth flow).

steps.action:
Optionally link the new user to one of the employee records
from P1-EMP-003.
steps.expected:
The link is recorded. The user record reflects the employee
linkage.

expected_overall:
A second user exists with a non-admin role and (optionally) is
linked to an employee record.
pass_criteria:
User exists with the assigned role AND can sign in (verify in
P1-USER-002) AND, if linked, the employee linkage is visible.

negative_variants.title: Reject invitation to deactivated role
negative_variants.action:
Deactivate or delete a non-admin role, then try to invite a
user assigned to that role.
negative_variants.expected:
The save is blocked with a clear message that the chosen role
is unavailable.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Invitation to a non-active role is refused.

negative_variants.title: Reject invite without email
negative_variants.action: Try to send an invite with the email field empty.
negative_variants.expected: Save is blocked with a clear "email is required" error.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Invite without email is refused.

title: Verify role-based access restricts what a non-admin user can see
goal:
Sign in as the user created in P1-USER-001 and confirm they cannot
see or change administrator-only settings.
preconditions: A second non-admin system user exists, assigned a Floor Operator (or equivalent) role and able to sign in. (Established by P1-USER-001.)
You can sign in as the user created in P1-USER-001 (a credential or invite link).

steps.action:
Sign out from the administrator account. Sign in as the second
user.
steps.expected:
Sign-in succeeds. The home / landing page is appropriate to a
Floor Operator (work order list, scanner workflow, etc.) — NOT
the administrator setup pages.

steps.action:
Try to navigate to the administrator-only areas: company
settings, user management, role permissions, integrations,
chart of accounts.
steps.expected:
Each area is either invisible in the navigation OR returns a
clear "you don't have permission" message — not a stack trace,
not a blank screen, not a partial page.

steps.action: Sign back out. Sign back in as the original administrator.
steps.expected: The administrator pages are visible and functional again.

expected_overall:
Role permissions actually restrict access. The non-admin user sees
only what their role allows; the administrator sees everything.
pass_criteria:
Each restricted area was inaccessible to the non-admin user AND
produced a clear, non-leaky error or omission.
why_this_matters:
Permissions checks that look right in the UI but don't actually
enforce on the backend are a common security bug — the UI hides
the menu but a direct URL still works. Spot-checking a few areas
in this case catches the most common version of that bug.

negative_variants.title: Direct URL access to a restricted page
negative_variants.action:
While signed in as the non-admin user, try to reach an
administrator-only page by typing its URL directly into the
browser address bar.
negative_variants.expected:
The page does NOT render. Either you're redirected to a
"permission denied" screen or back to your role's home page.
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Restricted page does not load AND no privileged data leaks
into the page that does load.

title: Deactivate a system user
goal:
Verify a user can be deactivated (so they can no longer sign in)
without losing their historical activity in the system.
preconditions: At least one non-admin user exists (P1-USER-001).

steps.action:
Open the user record from P1-USER-001. Find and use the
deactivate / disable action.
steps.expected: A confirmation dialog appears. Choose to confirm.

steps.action: Sign out and try to sign in as the deactivated user.
steps.expected:
Sign-in is rejected with a clear message — not a generic
"wrong password" error that would let an attacker enumerate
accounts.

steps.action:
Sign back in as the administrator. Search for the deactivated
user.
steps.expected:
The user still appears, marked as deactivated. Their
historical activity (audit log entries, anything attributed
to them) is preserved.

expected_overall:
Deactivated users cannot sign in but their records and history
remain. They can be reactivated if needed.
pass_criteria:
Sign-in is blocked AND the user record still exists AND
historical activity is preserved.
why_this_matters:
Deletion of users would orphan audit log entries and make it
impossible to reconstruct who did what. Deactivation preserves
the trail while preventing further access.

negative_variants.title: Cannot deactivate the last administrator
negative_variants.action:
As the only remaining administrator, try to deactivate
yourself.
negative_variants.expected:
The action is blocked with a clear message that at least one
active administrator must exist.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Last-admin deactivation is refused.

negative_variants.title: Deactivation revokes active sessions
negative_variants.action:
While Sam Rivera is signed in (in another browser), have the
admin deactivate Sam Rivera. Wait briefly and have Sam Rivera
try any action.
negative_variants.expected:
Sam Rivera's session is invalidated. Their next action returns
a sign-in prompt or "session ended" message.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Existing sessions of deactivated users do not continue to act.

title: Create the first work center
goal:
Configure a physical or logical area where production work happens.
Work orders will be assigned to work centers; labor and overhead
will post against them.
preconditions: At least one location exists.
You are signed in as a user with permission to configure work centers.

steps.action: Find and open the work centers area.
steps.expected: The work centers list appears, empty by default.

steps.action:
Choose the action to create a new work center. Enter:
- Name: "Press Shop"
- Location: {{primary_location}} - Main Plant
- Default labor rate: a plausible hourly rate
- Capacity: a plausible hours-per-day value
steps.expected: The form accepts the values and saves.

steps.action: Open the work center you just created.
steps.expected:
All values you entered are visible. The location is correctly
linked to the location record from P1-LOC-001.

expected_overall:
The work center exists, is linked to a location, and carries default
values that routings will inherit.
pass_criteria:
Work center is in the list AND opens to show entered values AND
links to the correct location.

negative_variants.title: Reject work center without a location
negative_variants.action: Try to create a work center with no location selected.
negative_variants.expected:
Submission is blocked. The error explains that a location is
required.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: Submission blocked AND error names the location field.

negative_variants.title: Reject negative capacity or labor rate
negative_variants.action:
Try to create a work center with capacity = -8 hours/day, then
again with labor rate = -$25.
negative_variants.expected:
Each submission is blocked with a plain-English error explaining
that the value must be non-negative. The application does NOT
silently accept a negative number that would later corrupt
capacity calculations.
negative_variants.pass_criteria:
Both submissions blocked AND error wording is clear AND no
record was created.

negative_variants.title: Reject work center pointing at an inactive location
negative_variants.action:
Deactivate a location (or attempt to point a new work center at
a known-deactivated location).
negative_variants.expected:
Selection is either filtered out or rejected with a clear
message that the destination location is inactive.
negative_variants.pass_criteria: No work center created against an inactive location.

title: Create work centers for common operations
goal:
Round out the work center list to cover the operations a typical
fabrication shop performs. Subsequent routings will assign operations
to these.
preconditions: At least one work center exists (e.g. Press Shop), linked to the primary location, with a default labor rate and capacity. (Established by P1-WC-001.)

steps.action:
Create a work center for each of the following:
- "Cut & Saw" (location: primary)
- "Weld" (location: primary)
- "Paint" (location: primary)
- "Assembly" (location: primary)
- "Inspection" (location: primary)
steps.expected: Each work center is created successfully and appears in the list.

steps.action: Open the work centers list and sort or filter by name.
steps.expected:
All five new work centers (plus Press Shop) are visible. Sort
and filter behave as a normal list view should.

expected_overall:
Six work centers exist, all linked to the primary location, ready to
be referenced by routings in Phase 2.
